bug_report.Title: Same pmxbot.logging source loaded as two module objects; core.initialize mutation not visible to tests
bug_report.Description: After switching import-mode to importlib, the same source file pmxbot/logging.py is imported twice under different module names, producing two distinct module objects. core.initialize() sets a class attribute (Logger.store) on one module object, but tests observe a different pmxbot.logging module object and get AttributeError when accessing Logger.store.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in pmxbot/core.initialize: initialize assumes the pmxbot.logging source is imported only once and that mutating pmxbot.logging.Logger on the module it imported will be universally visible. Location: pmxbot/core.initialize (method initialize). Evidence: tests call core.initialize(config) but then see logging.Logger without .store; PDB shows sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging -> False.

2) Incorrect assumption in pmxbot/logging.Logger: code assumes a single Logger class identity exists process-wide. Location: pmxbot/logging.Logger (class). Consequence: setting Logger.store on one Logger class object does not affect a second Logger class object created by a second import of the same source file.

3) Incorrect assumption in tests (import expectations): tests/unit/test_commands.py:setup_class assumes test imports resolve to the canonical pmxbot.logging module. Location: tests/unit/test_commands.py:setup_class (and any test modules that import 'logging' without package qualification or that may shadow names). Evidence: test immediately fails with AttributeError after core.initialize; PDB shows the test's logging module object is a different module object than sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'].

4) Incorrect runtime assumption about sys.modules keys / module identity: code assumes a single sys.modules key corresponds to a source file. Location: runtime module import resolution and any code that relies on module identity comparisons. Relevant relationship/formula observed for detecting duplicates: let logging_mod = importlib.import_module('pmxbot.logging'); canonical_file = getattr(logging_mod, '__file__', None). Then any module entry 'mod' in sys.modules whose getattr(mod, '__file__', None) == canonical_file but mod is not logging_mod represents the same source file loaded under a different module name. (i.e., duplicate detection: getattr(mod, '__file__', None) == canonical_file). The bug arises because these duplicates are treated as independent modules rather than the same module.

5) Incorrect assumption about module-name strings / loader behavior: some code (tests, dynamic loaders, or test configuration) uses ambiguous or non-package-qualified module names (e.g., 'logging' instead of 'pmxbot.logging') or file-based/dynamic loaders, which enables loading the same file under multiple module names. Location: places that call importlib, import by path, or use unqualified import strings (unknown exact files; investigation gap). Evidence: agent analysis flagged ambiguous module-name strings and possible use of dynamic loaders as likely contributors; PDB shows multiple module objects for same __file__.
StepsToReproduce: Switch the project to import-mode=importlib (as described in the original change).
Run the test suite (pytest) for the project (the failure appears during tests/unit/test_commands.py).

Observe failure in tests/unit/test_commands.py: setup_class calls core.initialize(config) and then logging.Logger.store.message(...), raising AttributeError: type object 'Logger' has no attribute 'store'.

Optionally, in a failing test run enter a debugger and inspect: logging (the test's local module), sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] and check whether sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging -> False and whether both modules have the same __file__.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: After core.initialize(config) completes, the Logger class exposed as pmxbot.logging.Logger (as imported by tests) has the class attribute .store set and is usable by tests; calling logging.Logger.store.message(...) in tests succeeds without AttributeError.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: core.initialize sets Logger.store on one module object, but tests import a different module object for the same source file and see no .store. Tests fail with AttributeError: type object 'Logger' has no attribute 'store'. PDB shows sys.modules['pmxbot.logging'] is logging -> False, indicating two distinct module objects pointing to the same file.

Action Input: {"problem": "Problem: Unroll the iterable for all/any calls to get better reports
Sometime I need to assert some predicate on all of an iterable, and for that the builtin functions `all`/`any` are great - but the failure messages aren't useful at all!
For example - the same test written in three ways:

- A generator expression
```sh                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
    def test_all_even():
        even_stevens = list(range(1,100,2))
>       assert all(is_even(number) for number in even_stevens)
E       assert False
E        +  where False = all(<generator object test_all_even.<locals>.<genexpr> at 0x101f82ed0>)
```
- A list comprehension
```sh
    def test_all_even():
        even_stevens = list(range(1,100,2))
>       assert all([is_even(number) for number in even_stevens])
E       assert False
E        +  where False = all([False, False, False, False, False, False, ...])
```
- A for loop
```sh
    def test_all_even():
        even_stevens = list(range(1,100,2))
        for number in even_stevens:
>           assert is_even(number)
E           assert False
E            +  where False = is_even(1)

test_all_any.py:7: AssertionError
```
The only one that gives a meaningful report is the for loop - but it's way more wordy, and `all` asserts don't translate to a for loop nicely (I'll have to write a `break` or a helper function - yuck)
I propose the assertion re-writer "unrolls" the iterator to the third form, and then uses the already existing reports.

For generator expressions inside all(), the call is to built-in 'all' with a generator argument. The visit_Call handles visiting args and function. It will call visit on the generator expression node — but there is no visit_GeneratorExp, so generic_visit will be used, which assigns the generator expression, then explanation displays its saferepr — which for generator is unhelpful, hence the output showing generator object.

Thought: Checkpoint 1: Identify the bug

- What I extracted from the report
  - The problem is that assertions using assert all(<generator expr>) produce very poor failure output:
    - For generator expressions: message shows the generator object (useless).
    - For list comprehensions: message shows the list of booleans (somewhat better).
    - For explicit for-loop + assert: message pinpoints the exact failing expression (best).
  - The feature/area implicated is pytest's "assertion rewriter" — the AST-based rewrite that turns assert X into expanded code that produces detailed failure reports.

- Tools used & why
  - I searched for assertion-rewriting code to find the code responsible for building messages. I located the relevant module:
    - src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
  - I opened that file to inspect the AssertionRewriter and its visit logic.
  - I also opened src/_pytest/assertion/util.py to see helper functions and understand how explanations are formatted and what helpers exist for builtins.

- Relevant locations found
  - Primary file/class responsible: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter
    - The methods visit_Call (implemented as visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy) handle call nodes.
    - When a Call arg is a generator expression (ast.GeneratorExp) the rewriter ultimately falls back to generic_visit which assigns the expression to a variable and displays its saferepr — for a generator that is the generator object, hence the poor message.
  - Helper utilities live in src/_pytest/assertion/util.py

- Clear statement of the bug and primary responsible code
  - Bug: assert all(<generator expression>) yields unhelpful failure messages because the assertion rewriter shows a generator object repr rather than the expanded boolean outcomes or the per-item failing predicate.
  - Primary responsible class/method: AssertionRewriter in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py, specifically its visit_Call implementation (visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy), and the way generator/list-comprehension args are handled (or not handled).

3a) Component classes referenced by primary class

3b) Parent class (ast.NodeVisitor)
- The parent is ast.NodeVisitor from the Python standard library. It provides the visit mechanism (visit(), generic_visit()) that AssertionRewriter overrides.
- The AssertionRewriter overrides generic_visit, and implements many visit_* methods for specific AST node types (Assert, Name, BoolOp, UnaryOp, BinOp, Call, Attribute, Compare, etc.).
- Methods in parent: visit, generic_visit, and dispatching behavior; AssertionRewriter overrides generic_visit and defines multiple visit_* methods for expressions it wants to specially handle. It inherits other behavior from ast.NodeVisitor but doesn't rely on additional parent methods beyond the dispatching.

Which of the parent's methods are overridden by AssertionRewriter:
- generic_visit (overridden)
- visit_* methods as listed above: visit_Assert, visit_Name, visit_BoolOp, visit_UnaryOp, visit_BinOp, visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy, visit_Starred, visit_Attribute, visit_Compare

Which methods are inherited without overriding:
- The base visit() dispatch method from ast.NodeVisitor is inherited and used to dispatch to visit_* methods. AssertionRewriter does not override visit() itself.

3c) Sibling classes inheriting from the same parent
- There are many NodeVisitor subclasses across the codebase, but within this module AssertionRewriter is the only NodeVisitor subclass. Other modules may define AST visitors differently; none are directly relevant to this feature.

3d) Call graph around primary class
- The AssertionRewritingHook calls rewrite_asserts, which constructs an AssertionRewriter and calls run(mod).
- run(mod) walks the AST and replaces ast.Assert nodes by calling self.visit on the assert node which triggers visit_Assert and many expression-visitors.
- visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy are invoked when an assert test contains a function call (e.g. all(...)).
- generic_visit handles expressions not specifically implemented (e.g. GeneratorExp, ListComp if there's no visit_ListComp implementation).

Observations that matter for the bug:
- There is no visit_GeneratorExp or visit_ListComp in AssertionRewriter, so generator/list comprehensions are handled by generic_visit, which assigns the expression to a temporary variable (self.assign) and uses self.display(res) to create an explanation param from saferepr of that temporary variable.
- For a generator expression, saferepr(temp_var) is the generator object's repr — which is unhelpful.
- For a list comprehension written explicitly as list([...]) or with brackets literal in the test, the resulting repr is a list of booleans and so the message is slightly more informative.

Conclusion from architecture:
- The simplest targeted change is to handle calls to builtins all() / any() when passed a generator expression (and perhaps also other comprehension types) specially: either expand them to a list first (so the explanation shows the expanded booleans) or unroll them into explicit per-item assertion(s) to get the same per-item message the for-loop gives.
- A minimal, safe, and targeted change is to coerce generator expressions passed to all()/any() into an explicit list call during AST visiting so that the rewriter will show the list of evaluated values rather than the generator object.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (concise):
- In AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy, detect when the call is to the builtin 'all' or 'any' and the single positional argument is an ast.GeneratorExp (a generator expression). Replace that single argument with a call to the builtin list (but using the @py_builtins alias the rewriter inserts) wrapping the original generator expression, i.e.:

    all((GENEXP))  ->  all(@py_builtins.list((GENEXP)))

- This preserves semantics (evaluates the generator into a list) and makes assertion messages more informative because a list's saferepr will show the booleans or results inside rather than the generator object's repr.
- Apply the same change in both visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy so both code paths are covered.

Why this change:
- It fixes the specific complaint: generator expressions passed to all()/any() produce a generator object's repr in messages. Converting to a list gives a helpful listing of booleans (like when the test author already wrote a list comprehension).
- It is a small, low-risk transformation localized to the call handling code (two places, for 3.5+ and legacy call signatures).
- It is backwards-compatible with the semantics of assert tests (it does force full evaluation of generator, but assertions would evaluate anyway in many contexts; for test failure reports we want the evaluation).

Now I work through the required questions:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current code assumes that expressions passed to call nodes should be left alone, and that the generic expression handling will produce a reasonable representation — in particular, it assumes not to modify generator expressions passed to builtins like all()/any(). My fix removes that assumption in the specific case of builtin all()/any(): it assumes it is acceptable and helpful to evaluate (materialize) generator expressions into lists for the purpose of producing better assertion messages.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- Hardcoded/implicit items I inspected:
  - There are no numeric constants encoding the generator vs list behavior.
  - The implicit assumption is encoded by the absence of a visit_GeneratorExp or any special-case for 'all'/'any' in visit_Call — i.e., the code path currently treats generators generically. So the "encoded assumption" is that the generic_visit behavior is fine for all expressions. That is represented in code by:
    - absence of visit_GeneratorExp method
    - visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy simply visiting args and building call results, without special-casing 'all'/'any' and GeneratorExp.
- Are any existing hardcoded values invalidated by the fix?
  - No existing integer or size constant must be changed.
  - The only change is generating a wrapper call to the builtin list; we must ensure we use the rewriter's @py_builtins alias so we don't accidentally reference a possibly shadowed 'list' in the user's module. The helper method builtin('list') returns ast.Attribute(ast.Name("@py_builtins"), 'list', ast.Load()), which is appropriate.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation would be reading/executing compiled rewritten code and interpreting the assertion failure — we must ensure that other parts of the rewriter that build explanations (pop_format_context, helper calls, variable clearing) still work with the new AST shape.
- The primary parent's methods to consider: AssertionRewriter.visit_Assert (we'll be using the same path because the Call node will now be an ast.Call where one arg is an ast.Call to @py_builtins.list). No change to visit_Assert is required because the top_condition returned by visiting the Call remains an expression assigned to a temporary variable, and the rest of the assertion-building logic (on_failure, explanation) remains valid.
- Concretely: after the change, a call like all(genexpr) becomes all(@py_builtins.list(genexpr)). visit_Call will:
  - visit the function name 'all' (as before)
  - visit the single arg (which is now a Call node to @py_builtins.list(genexpr)); visit on that inner Call will evaluate and assign the list to a temporary variable and produce an explanation (saferepr) for that list — exactly what we want.
- I inspected pop_format_context, helper, and display logic: they will continue to operate normally. The only new thing is an extra call node in the AST (list(...)), but AssertionRewriter already handles call nodes and will produce the more useful representation.

To be thorough, I re-check the parent's visit_Call implementation paths used:
- visit_Call_35/visit_Call_legacy build 'new_call = ast.Call(new_func, new_args, new_kwargs, ...)' and then
  - res = self.assign(new_call)
  - res_expl = self.explanation_param(self.display(res))

  -> This logic will naturally cause the inner list(...) call to be represented by its elements. No complementary operation (like eval) needs to be changed because we are just introducing an additional Call AST node which the rewriter already supports.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"

- visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy: they will be modified to pre-process generator arguments; otherwise logic remains unchanged.

I verified:
- The variable naming (self.variable(), temporary names like "@py_assert0") continues to work.
- The on_failure and pop_format_context flow is unchanged and will produce the formatted explanation that includes the list repr.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"

Change summary (every affected location):
1) Modify visit_Call_35 in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
   - Detect when the function being called is the builtin 'all' or 'any' and the single positional argument is an ast.GeneratorExp (generator expression). Replace that single argument with a call to builtin('list') with the generator expression as the argument.
   - This ensures generator expressions passed to all()/any() are materialized into a list before formatting, so the assertion message uses a list repr rather than the generator object repr.

2) Modify visit_Call_legacy in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py
   - Apply the same logic for older Python versions where visit_Call_legacy is used.

Exact code change sketch (where to insert):

- In visit_Call_35 (before new_func, func_expl = self.visit(call.func) or right after entering the method but before iterating args), insert:

    # Special-case common usage: all(<generator expr>) and any(<generator expr>)
    if isinstance(call.func, ast.Name) and call.func.id in ("all", "any") and len(call.args) == 1:
        arg0 = call.args[0]
        if isinstance(arg0, ast.GeneratorExp):

            list_call = ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg0], [])
            # for Python 3.5+ ast_Call signature matches
            call = ast.Call(call.func, [list_call], call.keywords)

    # Continue as before using this possibly-modified 'call' node

- In visit_Call_legacy, similarly before iterating args:

    if isinstance(call.func, ast.Name) and call.func.id in ("all", "any") and len(call.args) == 1:
        arg0 = call.args[0]
        if isinstance(arg0, ast.GeneratorExp):
            list_call = ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg0], [])
            # pass list_call into the later logic as the arg (adapt to legacy arg setup)

Notes about using self.builtin("list"):
- Using self.builtin("list") is preferable to using ast.Name("list") because the rewriter inserts an alias @py_builtins for the builtins module at the top of the rewritten module; referencing @py_builtins.list ensures we invoke the real builtin list even if the user has shadowed 'list' in their module.

Edge cases / additional considerations:
- Comprehensions other than ast.GeneratorExp: the code could also special-case other comprehension nodes (ListComp). However, explicit list comprehensions (using [ ... ]) are already good (their saferepr gives the list), so no change is necessary. The primary poor case is generator expressions (parentheses form) which evaluate to generator objects.
- Map/filter usage: some users might write all(map(func, iterable)). We could also special-case map/filter to produce better output (e.g., wrap map(...) in list), but the immediate bug report focuses on generator expressions. A follow-up patch could handle map/filter and other lazily-evaluated iterables the same way (coerce to list).
- Changing evaluation/time-of-side-effects: wrapping a generator into list changes when items are evaluated: previously generator evaluated lazily; after change the generator is fully consumed at that point. However, since the assert must evaluate it to produce the boolean anyway, the behavior for assertion semantics is consistent; this does not alter program semantics except for timing/ordering of side-effects that would be different if the test code relied on lazy behavior between asserts (unlikely). This is a conscious trade-off in favor of better failure messages. If this were a concern, a more elaborate transformation (unrolling into per-item asserts) would keep semantics more similar, but also be larger and more complex.

- After:

Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- The assertion rewriter did not special-case generator expressions passed to built-in all()/any() calls. Because AST visitor does not implement visit_GeneratorExp or a specific transformation in visit_Call for these cases, the generic expression handling produced a saferepr of the generator object, which is not helpful in assertion failure messages.

Every location that needs to change
- Primary locations to change (both must be updated):
  1) src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35
     - Current behavior: visits the call.func and call.args and treats generator expressions as generic expressions (generic_visit), leading to saferepr of generator object.
     - Change: detect builtins 'all'/'any' with a single argument that is ast.GeneratorExp and wrap that argument with a call to builtin('list') so the rewriter will present the list of evaluated values instead of the generator object.
  2) src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py :: AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_legacy
     - Same as above, for Python 3.4 and older call signature logic.

Why both locations:
- visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy handle different AST / interpreter versions; to remain correct across Python versions both branches must be patched.

What the current code does, what assumption it encodes, and what the fix should be
- Current code:
  - Assumes expressions are fine without special-casing lazy iterables like generator expressions passed to all()/any().
  - generic_visit assigns expressions to temporary variables and uses saferepr on them; for generator expressions, this prints the generator object.
- What assumption is changed:
  - We change the assumption that generator expressions passed to all/any should be left untouched for repr purposes. We now assume it's acceptable/preferable to materialize them to lists to give better diagnostic output.
- Fix:
  - Materialize generator arguments to all()/any() by wrapping them into a builtin('list') call in the AST rewriting step.

Hardcoded values to become dynamic
- No numeric hardcoded values in rewrite.py were encoding this assumption. No constants require changes.

Additional methods required
- No new methods required for the minimal fix. For a full "unroll into for-loop" implementation a new helper method would be needed and visit_Assert would need more complex rewriting. That is out of scope for this minimal fix.

Why this is safe / trade-offs
- Safety:
  - The change preserves the truth value semantics of all()/any(): all() and any() will still be called on an iterable of booleans/values. The only change is that the iterable (if it was a generator) will be fully realized into a list before all/any is applied. For assertion purposes, evaluating the generator is necessary to produce the debug output anyway. The call to the builtin('list') uses the rewriter's insertion of @py_builtins to avoid using a potentially shadowed 'list' name.
- Trade-offs:
  - Materializing generator expressions changes evaluation timing and memory behavior (full evaluation instead of lazy iteration). This is acceptable in the assertion context where the test writer requested all(...) and expects the collection to be used for the assertion result; however, if a generator has side-effects or yields very many items, tests may see different side effects or increased memory use. If preserving laziness is required, a more complex unroll/loop rewrite would be necessary.

Suggested patch (summary)
- Insert code in visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy to detect builtin all()/any() with a GeneratorExp arg and replace the arg with ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [original_generator_arg], []).

Testing notes
- Add tests showing:
  - assert all(f(x) for x in xs) fails: the message should include a list of boolean values rather than a generator object repr.
  - assert any(f(x) for x in xs) similarly.
  - Ensure no regressions for normal calls, nested calls, or explicit list comprehensions.
  - Consider adding tests for map/filter as a future enhancement.

If you want, I can produce the exact patch (a diff) to apply to src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py with the precise insertion points for both visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy.

- Bug: assert all(<generator expr>) / any(<generator expr>) produce useless failure output because the assertion rewriter displays the generator object instead of the evaluated contents; the wrong assumption is that generic expression handling is sufficient for lazy iterables.

The code lives in src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py — class AssertionRewriter (methods visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy) plus the generic_visit behavior that assigns expressions and uses saferepr. (Findings derived from Checkpoints 1–3.)
- Proposed fix: in AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35 and visit_Call_legacy, detect builtin all/any with a single ast.GeneratorExp argument and replace that argument with a call to self.builtin("list")(original_generator). Concretely: wrap the GeneratorExp in ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg], []). This change must be applied in both call-handling methods to cover all supported AST code paths. (Checkpoint 4.)
- Hardcoded values/constants: none numeric; the implicit hardcoded assumption is “do not special-case GeneratorExp”—this is removed. Use self.builtin("list") (already available) rather than ast.Name("list") to avoid user shadowing.
- Methods to add/override: none required for the minimal fix. A larger alternative ("unroll into explicit for-loop + per-item assert") would need new helper code and more invasive rewriting; out of scope for the minimal patch. (Checkpoint 4.)
- Investigation gaps: the agent did not (a) test the change against other lazy iterables (map/filter/itertools.*), (b) check calls where the callee is an attribute (e.g., module.all) or a shadowed name beyond treating builtin('list'), and (c) run concrete end-to-end examples showing the new output or run unit tests. What: AssertionRewriter does not implement visit_GeneratorExp and does not special-case GeneratorExp when it appears as an argument to calls; generic_visit assigns the expression to a temp variable and uses saferepr, which yields the generator object repr instead of useful contents.

This uses the rewriter’s @py_builtins alias to avoid shadowing. It’s small, localized, and covers the immediate complaint.

If full preservation of laziness is required, a more invasive "unroll to for-loop + per-item assert" rewrite is necessary.

bug_report.Title: Assertion rewriter shows generator object for all()/any(generator expr) leading to unhelpful failure messages
bug_report.Description: When an assertion uses all(...) or any(...) with a generator expression argument, the pytest assertion rewriter displays the generator object (<generator object ...>) instead of the evaluated contents or the failing element, producing an unhelpful failure message.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: generic_visit's strategy (assign expression to a temp and display via saferepr) is adequate for all expression types, including ast.GeneratorExp. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.generic_visit. Evidence: generator args are assigned to temps and their saferepr yields '<generator object ...>'.

2) Incorrect assumption: call-handling need not special-case generator expressions. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_35. Evidence/relationship: visit_Call_35 treats call.args generically; when the callee is builtin all/any and the single argument is ast.GeneratorExp, the argument is left as-is and later displayed as a generator object. The investigation specifies the relationship that should be used to obtain useful diagnostics: wrap the GeneratorExp in ast.Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg], []).

3) Incorrect assumption: legacy call handling is already correct. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.visit_Call_legacy. Evidence: visit_Call_legacy mirrors visit_Call_35 and likewise leaves GeneratorExp unwrapped; the same wrapping/handling is required on this code path to cover older AST shapes.

4) Incorrect assumption: constructing a list call with ast.Name("list") is safe (i.e., unqualified 'list' will always refer to the builtin). Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - AssertionRewriter.builtin and call sites. Evidence/relationship: the rewriter must reference its injected builtin alias (self.builtin("list")) to avoid user shadowing; the recommended wrapper is ast_Call(self.builtin("list"), [arg], []).

5) Incorrect assumption: changing diagnostics can be done without changing evaluation semantics. Location: src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py - visit_Call_35 / visit_Call_legacy (design implication). Evidence: materializing a GeneratorExp with list() forces full evaluation and alters timing/memory and side-effect ordering; this behavioral trade-off is a necessary consequence of the minimal diagnostic change and must be acknowledged/tested.
StepsToReproduce: Run pytest on the test file (e.g., `pytest test_file.py`).
Observe the assertion failure output for the all(...) / any(...) assertion.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When an all(...) or any(...) assertion using a generator expression fails, the pytest assertion output should show useful information about the failing element(s) or the evaluated contents (e.g., the specific item/value that caused the assertion to fail), similar in informativeness to the equivalent for-loop assertion.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior:
pytest prints an unhelpful failure message showing the generator object, e.g.:

E   assert False
E    +  where False = all(<generator object ...>)

which does not reveal which item failed or the evaluated values.

  - pytest --fixtures (function _showfixtures_main)
  - pytest --fixtures-per-test (function _show_fixtures_per_test)

Why both places? Because there are two separate commands / code paths that print fixtures:
- _showfixtures_main prints "all available fixtures"
- _show_fixtures_per_test prints fixtures used by each test

This addresses the feature request comprehensively.

Now, challenge the proposed fix by answering 4a..4e.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence answer: The current code assumes that fixture listings need not display fixture scope; the fix changes this by assuming the scope string (fixturedef.scope) is available and should be shown alongside the fixture name.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required. Both printing functions are updated to include scope, and no other helper or override is necessary.

If desired, we could factor out a small helper format_fixture_display(fixturedef, bestrel, verbose) to keep DRY, but it's not required for correctness.

Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause, all locations to change, and the fix

Root cause
- The code paths that print fixture lists (_showfixtures_main and _show_fixtures_per_test in src/_pytest/python.py) never formatted or displayed the fixture scope attribute stored on FixtureDef. The scope is available (FixtureDef.scope) but omitted from user-facing output. The bug is simply an omission in the formatting used when printing fixture entries.

Every location that needs to change
- src/_pytest/python.py:
  1) _show_fixtures_per_test: inner function write_fixture — it builds funcargspec and prints fixture name and optionally bestrel; must include scope.
     - Current behavior: prints "argname" (or "argname -- path" when verbose).
     - Assumption encoded: scope is not shown.
     - Fix: include scope string, e.g. "argname (scope) -- path" in verbose mode, "argname (scope)" in non-verbose.
  2) _showfixtures_main: in the main loop that prints the available fixtures — build and print funcargspec similarly to include scope.
     - Current behavior: prints "argname" (or "argname -- path" when verbose).
     - Fix: include scope string: "%s (%s) -- %s" etc.

- No changes are needed in src/_pytest/fixtures.py (FixtureDef already stores scope), but we must ensure we read scope via fixturedef.scope (or getattr defensively) so the code won't break for pseudo objects.

Patch (conceptual, minimal changes) — two small edits in src/_pytest/python.py.

1) _show_fixtures_per_test.write_fixture (replace the formatting portion):

Before:
```
    if verbose > 0:
        bestrel = get_best_relpath(fixture_def.func)
        funcargspec = "{} -- {}".format(argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

After:
```
    scope = getattr(fixture_def, "scope", None) or ""
    if verbose > 0:
        bestrel = get_best_relpath(fixture_def.func)
        funcargspec = "{} ({}) -- {}".format(argname, scope, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = "{} ({})".format(argname, scope)
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

2) _showfixtures_main main loop (replace the formatting portion):

Before:
```
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s -- %s" % (argname, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = argname
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

After:
```
    scope = getattr(fixturedef, "scope", None) or ""
    if verbose > 0:
        funcargspec = "%s (%s) -- %s" % (argname, scope, bestrel)
    else:
        funcargspec = "%s (%s)" % (argname, scope)
    tw.line(funcargspec, green=True)
```

Rationale for defensiveness:
- I use getattr(fixturedef, "scope", None) because in the wild there are PseudoFixtureDef objects and other wrappers; although the fixtures printed by _showfixtures_main come from FixtureDef lists in FixtureManager, being defensive avoids unexpected AttributeError. PseudoFixtureDef has scope so this is extra safe.

Effects on tests and other code
- Existing tests in the test suite assert printed fixture-related strings using fnmatch, not exact matching; the new output contains the previous substrings (fixture names, docstrings, and "-- <path>") and additionally includes the scope in parentheses. The existing fnmatch patterns are permissive and will still match; hence tests should continue to pass.
- No other code parses the fixture listing output, so no reverse/parsing changes are needed.
- No behavior of fixture execution, lifetime, or semantics is changed.

Why this is a complete fix
- Both entry points that display fixtures are updated:
  - The global fixtures listing (pytest --fixtures)
  - The per-test fixtures listing (pytest --fixtures-per-test)
- The data source for scope (FixtureDef.scope) is already present and used elsewhere; no modifications to how scope is stored, validated, or used in fixture lifetimes are necessary.
- I included a defensive getattr to avoid unforeseen attribute errors.

Suggested small refactor (optional)
- If desired, factor the formatting into a small helper:
  - def format_fixture_display(name, scope, bestrel=None, verbose=False): ...
  - Replace duplicated code in both write_fixture sites.
- Not necessary for correctness, but it reduces duplication.

If you want, I can:
- Produce a patch/diff (git unified diff) with the exact edits to src/_pytest/python.py.
- Run through the tests that reference fixture output (or list the updated test expectations if you prefer to make the scope presence explicit in tests).

Summary
- Root cause: printing functions omitted fixture.scope when showing fixtures.
- Fix: include fixture.scope in printed output in both _showfixtures_main and _show_fixtures_per_test.
- Locations changed: two small formatting edits in src/_pytest/python.py.
- No change to fixture management behavior or internal constants; no additional overrides needed.

This covers both global and per-test fixture listings. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4.)
- Constants/hardcoded values: no constants need to change; the canonical scopes list is in src/_pytest/fixtures.py: scopes = "session package module class function".split(). The fix reads the existing scope strings — no reorder or constant modification required. (Verified in Checkpoint 3.)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Printing omits fixture scope (root cause)
What: _showfixtures_main in src/_pytest/python.py builds funcargspec as either "argname" or "argname -- path" and never includes fixturedef.scope. This directly causes --fixtures to omit scope. (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 2.)

### Finding 2: Per-test fixture listing also omits scope
What: show_fixtures_per_test (src/_pytest/python.py) contains an inner write_fixture that formats fixture names the same way and omits scope for fixtures used by a test. Both listing code paths must be changed. (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 2.)

### Finding 3: Scope is available on fixture objects
What: FixtureDef objects in src/_pytest/fixtures.py include a scope attribute (FixtureDef.scope) and PseudoFixtureDef also carries scope — the data needed to display scope already exists and should be used. (Found in Checkpoint 2/3.)

### Finding 4: Canonical scope strings and ordering are defined and must be preserved
What: The canonical scope names are defined as scopes = "session package module class function".split() in src/_pytest/fixtures.py; other code uses these strings/indexes, so the fix should display the existing string and not change enum/order. (Found in Checkpoint 3.)

### Finding 5: Tests likely unaffected but were not executed
What: Existing tests in testing/python/fixtures.py match fixture output with fnmatch patterns (e.g. "*tmpdir*--*tmpdir.py*"), which are permissive and should still match after inserting "(scope)" between name and "--". However the agent did not run tests; a few tests might match exact strings elsewhere and may need updates. (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 4.)

### Finding 6: No reverse/parse dependency detected
What: No code parses the printed fixture listings; the output is for humans and tests only. So showing scope is not expected to break program logic, but the agent did not check docs/man pages or downstream parsers. (Found in Checkpoint 4c / 4e.)

### Finding 7: Minimal change required; be defensive against wrapper objects
What: Use getattr(fixturedef, "scope", "") when formatting to avoid AttributeError for any non-FixtureDef wrappers (defensive change). No new helper method required but factoring formatting into a small helper is optional. (Found in Checkpoint 4.)

===LOW_LEVEL===

- Related locations: src/_pytest/python.py:_showfixtures_main and tests in testing/python/fixtures.py that assert printed output.

### Location: src/_pytest/fixtures.py:FixtureDef.scope

bug_report.Title: pytest --fixtures and --fixtures-per-test omit fixture scope in listings
bug_report.Description: When running pytest --fixtures or pytest --fixtures-per-test, the printed fixture listings do not include each fixture's scope (session/package/module/class/function); only the fixture name (and path in verbose mode) are shown.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/python.py:_showfixtures_main encodes the assumption that fixture listings only need to display the fixture name (or "name -- path" in verbose mode) and never references FixtureDef.scope, causing global fixture listings to omit scope.

2) src/_pytest/python.py:show_fixtures_per_test -> write_fixture (nested function) encodes the same assumption for per-test listings: it builds funcargspec as "argname" or "argname -- path" and never references fixture.scope, causing per-test fixture listings to omit scope.

3) src/_pytest/fixtures.py:FixtureDef.scope (and PseudoFixtureDef.scope) already store the fixture scope; the canonical scope strings and ordering are defined as scopes = "session package module class function".split(). The omission in printing code is an incorrect assumption because the scope data is present and canonical.

4) testing/python/fixtures.py contains tests that use permissive fnmatch patterns that implicitly assume the absence of an inserted scope token in printed output; these tests encode an assumption about the current output format and may need review if the printed format changes.

5) The printing code assumes direct access to a scope attribute on fixture-like objects and does not defensively guard attribute access; wrapper or non-standard fixture objects could lack a scope attribute unless formatting uses a defensive access pattern (e.g. getattr(fixturedef, "scope", "")).
StepsToReproduce: Have a test project with at least one fixture defined (any scope).
Run: pytest --fixtures and inspect the fixture listing output; observe that each fixture shows only its name (and path in verbose output) but not the fixture scope.
Run: pytest --fixtures-per-test and inspect per-test fixture listings; observe that fixture scopes are also omitted there.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: pytest --fixtures and pytest --fixtures-per-test should list each fixture and include its scope string (one of: session, package, module, class, function) in the human-readable fixture listing so users can see the scope alongside the fixture name and any path information.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Fixture listings printed by pytest --fixtures and pytest --fixtures-per-test show only the fixture name or "name -- path" (in verbose mode) and do not include the fixture's scope; scope values are present on FixtureDef objects but are not printed.

method_cache.testing/logging/test_formatter.py:
import logging

import py.io

from _pytest.logging import ColoredLevelFormatter


def test_coloredlogformatter():
    logfmt = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s"

    record = logging.LogRecord(
        name="dummy",
        level=logging.INFO,
        pathname="dummypath",
        lineno=10,
        msg="Test Message",
        args=(),
        exc_info=False,
    )

    class ColorConfig(object):
        class option(object):
            pass

    tw = py.io.TerminalWriter()
    tw.hasmarkup = True
    formatter = ColoredLevelFormatter(tw, logfmt)
    output = formatter.format(record)
    assert output == (
        "dummypath                   10 \x1b[32mINFO    \x1b[0m Test Message"
    )

    tw.hasmarkup = False
    formatter = ColoredLevelFormatter(tw, logfmt)
    output = formatter.format(record)
    assert output == ("dummypath                   10 INFO     Test Message")

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (concrete change)
- Change DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT in src/_pytest/logging.py from:
  "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s"
  to:
  "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s"

Rationale:
- This inserts the logger name (%(name)s) before filename, as requested.
- It preserves the "%(levelname)-8s" token so ColoredLevelFormatter's regex continues to work.
- It removes the fixed width for filename and lineno (the proposed string uses no fixed width for filename and uses simple %d for lineno). This changes alignment but gives more useful module context (logger name).
- LoggingPlugin uses this constant as default for --log-format and log file default, so this update will change default behavior everywhere consistently.

Patch (one-line change)
File: src/_pytest/logging.py
Change:
- DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s"
+ DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT = "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s"

I would submit this commit that changes that single line.

Now I will answer the required follow-up questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Previous assumption: The base filename and line number alone were sufficient for identifying where log messages originated; formatting aligned columns using fixed widths was preferable. New assumption: The logger name (usually module/package name) is more useful than just the base filename, so it should be included in the default format; fixed-width alignment for filename/lineno is not necessary in the default.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The old DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT encoded alignment/width assumptions via:
  - "%(filename)-25s" (pad/trim filename to 25 chars)
  - "%(lineno)4d" (lineno width 4)
  - "%(levelname)-8s" (levelname width 8)

Evaluation:
- "%(levelname)-8s" remains in the new default and continues to be correct (ColoredLevelFormatter depends on a "%(levelname)...s" token and its regex will still match).
- "%(filename)-25s" and "%(lineno)4d" encoded a layout-focused assumption. The new default removes those width/column constraints and instead uses "%(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d". Because we added %(name)s, the previous fixed widths for alignment of the filename field are no longer appropriate; they were encoding a positional layout that assumed filename was the key identifier. Since the new assumption moves emphasis to logger name, the old width constraints are removed — this is an intentional change in formatting choice. Wherever code depended on the fixed-width format (e.g., precise column positions), that code would need updating — I searched the repository and found no code parsing formatted logs or requiring exact column positions (only a unit test that constructs the old string explicitly to test ColoredLevelFormatter). Therefore, no other hardcoded numeric values need to be made dynamic in the repo.

Additionally:
- LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX in ColoredLevelFormatter is r"%\(levelname\)([+-]?\d*s)". It expects the levelname to be followed by a width specifier (or none). Since we kept "%(levelname)-8s" in the new default, the regex still matches. If someone chose a default without a width spec for levelname, the regex would not match, and ColoredLevelFormatter would simply not colorize the level part (correct fallback). So no change required there.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The change alters how messages are formatted (writing). Is there any code that reads/parses formatted log output? I searched the repository for code that parses formatted logs or relies on their exact string layout; none found.
- Complementary operations to be checked: LogCaptureFixture.record_tuples and messages:
  - record_tuples returns tuples (r.name, r.levelno, r.getMessage()) — this is independent of formatting and will remain correct.
  - caplog.text or LogCaptureHandler.stream.getvalue() returns formatted text produced by the formatter. Changing DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT simply changes that text; no code reads it expecting an older format.
- Parent class logging.Formatter methods (format, formatTime, formatException) would be used for formatting; adding %(name)s only adds a field that logging.Formatter knows how to substitute. No change to logging.Formatter is required.

Concrete substitution check:
- In LoggingPlugin.__init__, formatter = logging.Formatter(get_option_ini(config, "log_format"), get_option_ini(config, "log_date_format"))
  - With no user override, get_option_ini returns DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT (the updated constant).
  - logging.Formatter will, for each LogRecord r, substitute:
    - levelname: r.levelname (string)
    - name: r.name (logger name)
    - filename: r.filename (base filename of caller)
    - lineno: r.lineno (line number)
    - message: result of r.getMessage()
  - All these attributes exist on LogRecord; no breakage.

Therefore, no complementary reading/parsing code needs changes.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- ColoredLevelFormatter.__init__ and format:
  - It searches for LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX inside self._fmt. Since new default still contains "%(levelname)-8s", LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX matches and the formatting remains colorized as before.
  - If a user supplies a format without a levelname width, ColoredLevelFormatter just won't apply colorization, same as before.
- LoggingPlugin.__init__ and other methods:
  - They only rely on logging.Formatter to construct formatters using the format string; switching the default string is safe.
- LogCaptureHandler, LogCaptureFixture, _LiveLoggingStreamHandler:
  - They don't rely on the exact format of the formatted message. They only emit or capture formatted text. All will continue to work.
- The test testing/logging/test_formatter.py:
  - This test defines its own local logfmt variable set to the old format string. It tests ColoredLevelFormatter behavior for that explicit format. That test will continue to pass unchanged. (It is not testing DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT.)

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required.
- Considerations:
  - If we wanted optional substitution of VIRTUAL_ENV in absolute filenames for brevity (the reporter suggested substituting $VIRTUAL_ENV), that would require new logic (a custom Formatter subclass to rewrite record.pathname or filename). But that is out-of-scope for this change; the issue only requested inclusion of %(name)s.
  - No inherited method from a parent needs to be overridden because we aren't changing how formatters format records beyond providing a new default format string.

Checkpoint 5: Final report

Root cause
- The default logging format (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT) in src/_pytest/logging.py does not include the logger name (%(name)s). For large projects the base filename alone is often insufficient to identify the source module; the logger name (module path) is much more informative. The code encoded a layout-focused assumption (fixed width filename and line number) that prioritized alignment over contextual information.

Every location that needs to change
- Primary change:
  - src/_pytest/logging.py:
    - Replace the DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT string as follows:
      - From: "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s"
      - To:   "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s"
    - Reason: Use logger name for better context and keep levelname token to keep colorization working.
    - Effect: all uses of DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT (command-line default of --log-format and file logging default) will pick up new format.

- Files to inspect/considered:
  - testing/logging/test_formatter.py contains a test that uses the old format string as an explicit test input. It does not need to be changed because it is explicitly testing formatting behavior for that exact format. If maintainers want the tests to exercise the new default, the test could be added or modified, but not required for correctness.

Why no other changes:
- ColoredLevelFormatter uses LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX to detect "%(levelname)...s" and will continue to work because "%(levelname)-8s" is still present in the new default.
- logging.Formatter accepts %(name)s and all other fields used in the new format; these are attributes on LogRecord.
- No code parses formatted log lines or relies on column positions produced by the previous fixed-width format (search showed no such code).
- record_tuples, caplog.text and other capture/inspection code does not depend on formatted string layout.

Possible follow-ups (not required for this change but worth considering)
- If maintainers prefer to keep column alignment, consider using formatted width tokens while including %(name)s. For instance:
  "%(name)-20s %(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" but be aware that long names will overflow alignment.
- Add an optional feature to shorten or rewrite file paths, e.g., replace $VIRTUAL_ENV prefix with $VIRTUAL_ENV or show relative paths — this would require a custom Formatter subclass that rewrites record.pathname/record.filename before formatting, and tests to cover it.
- Update documentation / release notes to mention the new default logging format.

Concrete patch (single-line change)
- src/_pytest/logging.py
  - line 18:
    - DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT = "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s"

I have reviewed usages and tests in the repository: no additional code needs to be changed for correctness. If you want, I can produce the exact diff patch in unified format or open a follow-up change to also add a test that asserts that the default log_format now contains %(name)s.

===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: The repository default log format (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT) omits the logger name (%(name)s), relying only on %(filename)s and %(lineno)d; that layout assumption makes it hard to identify the source module in large projects.
- Root cause & location: module-level constant DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT in src/_pytest/logging.py (line 18) encodes the wrong assumption and is used as the default for all formatters created by LoggingPlugin.
- Proposed fix: replace the single constant value with a format that includes %(name)s (suggested: "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s"). This one-line change covers all affected locations because DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is referenced as the single source of truth for defaults.
- Hardcoded values to change: the DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT string only. Keep "%(levelname)-8s" so ColoredLevelFormatter’s regex still matches.
- Methods to add/override: none required for this change. (Optional future work: a custom Formatter to shorten paths or substitute $VIRTUAL_ENV, which would require new code.)
- Investigation gaps: the agent did not inspect the stdlib logging.Formatter implementation byte-for-byte or run the test-suite with the new default; also did not check external plugins/tools that may parse formatted log text. These omissions were noted and flagged.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is the single wrong assumption source (Found at Checkpoint 1/2)
DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is defined in src/_pytest/logging.py as the literal:
"%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s". This hardcodes a filename-first, fixed-width layout and omits %(name)s (logger name) which the reporter requested; LoggingPlugin and pytest_addoption use this constant as the default log format.

### Finding 2: ColoredLevelFormatter expects a levelname width token and must remain compatible (Found at Checkpoint 2)
ColoredLevelFormatter.LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX = re.compile(r"%\(levelname\)([+-]?\d*s)") — the formatter looks for a "%(levelname)...s" token to insert colorized levelname fragments. The proposed new default keeps "%(levelname)-8s" so colorization logic still applies.

### Finding 3: Tests reference the old format literal but do not depend on DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT (Found at Checkpoint 1/2)
testing/logging/test_formatter.py defines logfmt = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" and exercises ColoredLevelFormatter with that explicit string. Since the test uses its own literal, changing DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT does not automatically break this test; no test change is strictly required.

### Finding 4: No code in repo parses formatted log output (Found at Checkpoint 3)
A repository-wide search found no consumers that parse the produced formatted log strings or rely on fixed column positions. Therefore changing the format string will not silently break local code that depended on exact column layout. However, the agent did not check external tooling/plugins outside the repo (gap).

### Finding 5: Concrete one-line fix (Found at Checkpoint 4)
Change the constant in src/_pytest/logging.py from
"%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s"
to
"%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s".
This preserves "%(levelname)-8s" (keeps color regex working) and adds %(name)s in front of filename for better context.

### Finding 6: Investigation omissions to address before merge (Found at Checkpoint 4)
The agent did not (a) inspect stdlib logging.Formatter source to trace exact substitution behavior with concrete LogRecord values, (b) run the test suite to spot any formatting-dependent assertions beyond the one located test, or (c) search for external plugins/tools that might parse log text. These are potential risks to validate before committing.

===LOW_LEVEL===

- **Wrong assumption**: The default assumes base filename + line number (and fixed widths) are sufficient identifiers; it omits the logger name (%(name)s).

bug_report.Title: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT omits logger name (%(name)s) — hardcoded format in src/_pytest/logging.py
bug_report.Description: The repository-wide default log format constant (DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT) in src/_pytest/logging.py hardcodes a filename-first, fixed-width layout and omits the logger name (%(name)s). As a result, formatted log lines lack module/logger context, making it hard to identify the source module in large projects.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT hardcoded value (src/_pytest/logging.py:DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT) encodes the incorrect assumption that the base filename + line number (and fixed-width alignment) are sufficient identifiers. Evidence: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s". Correct relationship: the format should include the logger name (%(name)s) and preserve the levelname width token ("%(levelname)-8s"); example replacement formula found in investigation: "%(levelname)-8s %(name)s:%(filename)s:%(lineno)d %(message)s".

2) ColoredLevelFormatter compatibility requirement (src/_pytest/logging.py:ColoredLevelFormatter.LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX). The formatter expects a "%(levelname)...s" token (regex r"%\(levelname\)([+-]?\d*s)") to insert colored levelname fragments. Incorrect assumption: changing DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT must not remove or break the "% (levelname)-8s" token; evidence: LEVELNAME_FMT_REGEX is used to locate levelname width specifiers.

3) LoggingPlugin default usage (src/_pytest/logging.py:LoggingPlugin.__init__). Incorrect assumption encoded: DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT is authoritative and appropriate for all default formatters created by LoggingPlugin. Evidence: the plugin constructs logging.Formatter using get_option_ini(config, "log_format") whose default is DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT, so the constant's value affects all default logger outputs and the CLI --log-format default.

4) Tests use the old literal format but are not coupled to the constant (testing/logging/test_formatter.py). The test defines logfmt = "%(filename)-25s %(lineno)4d %(levelname)-8s %(message)s" and exercises ColoredLevelFormatter with that explicit string. This is not an internal dependency on DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT, but it means tests asserting exact old-layout formatting exist and should be considered when changing defaults.

5) Investigation omissions that affect confidence in the change: the agent did not run the full test suite with the updated DEFAULT_LOG_FORMAT, did not inspect the stdlib logging.Formatter substitution behavior byte-for-byte, and did not search for external plugins/tools outside the repo that may parse formatted log text. These are potential risks if external consumers relied on the old column layout.
StepsToReproduce: Add or use a module that logs a message (e.g., a DEBUG log in utils.py).
Run pytest with default logging settings (no custom --log-format), e.g. `pytest`.
Observe the formatted log lines produced by pytest: they begin with the base filename and line number and do not include the logger/module name.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Default-formatted log lines should include the logger name (module name) so messages identify their source module. Example user-visible format: "DEBUG    django.db.backends:utils.py:114 (0.000) SELECT ..." (logger name before filename).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Log lines formatted with the current default look like: "utils.py                   114 DEBUG    (0.000) SELECT ..." or "multipart.py               604 DEBUG    Calling on_field_start with no data" — they start with filename and lineno and do not show the logger name/module.

<ExceptionInfo ...>

    The form above is fully supported but discouraged for new code because the
    context manager form is regarded as more readable and less error-prone.

    .. note::
        Similar to caught exception objects in Python, explicitly clearing
        local references to returned ``ExceptionInfo`` objects can
        help the Python interpreter speed up its garbage collection.

        Clearing those references breaks a reference cycle
        (``ExceptionInfo`` --> caught exception --> frame stack raising
        the exception --> current frame stack --> local variables -->
        ``ExceptionInfo``) which makes Python keep all objects referenced
        from that cycle (including all local variables in the current
        frame) alive until the next cyclic garbage collection run. See the
        official Python ``try`` statement documentation for more detailed
        information.

    """
    __tracebackhide__ = True
    for exc in filterfalse(isclass, always_iterable(expected_exception, BASE_TYPE)):
        msg = (
            "exceptions must be old-style classes or"
            " derived from BaseException, not %s"
        )
        raise TypeError(msg % type(exc))

    message = "DID NOT RAISE {}".format(expected_exception)
    match_expr = None

    if not args:
        if "message" in kwargs:
            message = kwargs.pop("message")
            warnings.warn(deprecated.RAISES_MESSAGE_PARAMETER, stacklevel=2)
        if "match" in kwargs:
            match_expr = kwargs.pop("match")
        if kwargs:
            msg = "Unexpected keyword arguments passed to pytest.raises: "
            msg += ", ".join(sorted(kwargs))
            raise TypeError(msg)
        return RaisesContext(expected_exception, message, match_expr)
    elif isinstance(args[0], str):
        warnings.warn(deprecated.RAISES_EXEC, stacklevel=2)
        code, = args
        assert isinstance(code, str)
        frame = sys._getframe(1)
        loc = frame.f_locals.copy()
        loc.update(kwargs)
        # print "raises frame scope: %r" % frame.f_locals
        try:
            code = _pytest._code.Source(code).compile(_genframe=frame)
            exec(code, frame.f_globals, loc)
            # XXX didn't mean f_globals == f_locals something special?
            #     this is destroyed here ...
        except expected_exception:
            return _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.from_current()
    else:
        func = args[0]
        try:
            func(*args[1:], **kwargs)
        except expected_exception:
            return _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.from_current()
    fail(message)


raises.Exception = fail.Exception


class RaisesContext:
    def __init__(self, expected_exception, message, match_expr):
        self.expected_exception = expected_exception
        self.message = message
        self.match_expr = match_expr
        self.excinfo = None

    def __enter__(self):
        self.excinfo = _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.for_later()
        return self.excinfo

    def __exit__(self, *tp):
        __tracebackhide__ = True
        if tp[0] is None:
            fail(self.message)
        self.excinfo.__init__(tp)
        suppress_exception = issubclass(self.excinfo.type, self.expected_exception)
        if self.match_expr is not None and suppress_exception:
            self.excinfo.match(self.match_expr)
        return suppress_exception

def test_entrysource_Queue_example():
    try:
        queue.Queue().get(timeout=0.001)
    except queue.Empty:
        excinfo = _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.from_current()
    entry = excinfo.traceback[-1]
    source = entry.getsource()
    assert source is not None
    s = str(source).strip()
    assert s.startswith("def get")


def test_codepath_Queue_example():
    try:
        queue.Queue().get(timeout=0.001)
    except queue.Empty:
        excinfo = _pytest._code.ExceptionInfo.from_current()
    entry = excinfo.traceback[-1]
    path = entry.path
    assert isinstance(path, py.path.local)
    assert path.basename.lower() == "queue.py"
    assert path.check()

That explains why str(e) prints "<console>:3: LookupError: A" — it uses only the first line of exconly (format_exception_only) and possibly truncates at newline. Need to check exconly implementation: exconly uses format_exception_only and rstrip — that returns the full message including newlines? format_exception_only for LookupError with message containing newlines probably returns 'LookupError: A\n'?? Since exconly joins lines and rstrip removes trailing newline, so exconly returns single-line? But earlier they got 'LookupError: A' without rest 'B\nC'. Why? But the ReprFileLocation.to terminal takes message and truncates at first newline (i = msg.find("\n"); if i != -1: msg = msg[:i]) before printing. So ReprFileLocation limits message to first line. Thought: Checkpoint 1: Identify the bug

1a) classify_report
- Reason: extract the entities referenced in the bug report so I know where to start.
- Result: The report pointed to str(e) on pytest.raises context variable, the ExceptionInfo object returned by pytest.raises, exconly(), and that str(e.value) works correctly. Entities of interest: ExceptionInfo, exconly, ReprFileLocation (inferred), ExceptionInfo.__str__.

Analysis: The user-observed difference is between str(e) where e is an ExceptionInfo (pytest.raises context) vs str(e) where e is the native exception instance. So the code path handling ExceptionInfo.__str__ / exconly / ReprFileLocation is the likely place.

1b) search_codebase for candidates

1d) Statement of the bug and primary location
- What the bug is: str(excinfo) (where excinfo is an ExceptionInfo instance from pytest.raises) returns a file:lineno plus only the first line of the exception text, because ExceptionInfo.__str__ uses ReprFileLocation which truncates the exception message at the first newline. That causes multi-line exception messages to appear truncated when printed with str(excinfo). In contrast, printing the actual exception instance (str(excinfo.value)) prints the full multi-line message.
- Primary class/method responsible: ExceptionInfo.__str__ in src/_pytest/_code/code.py (and the interaction with ReprFileLocation.toterminal() which truncates at the first newline).
