def pytest_addoption(parser: Parser) -> None:
    group = parser.getgroup("general")
    group.addoption(
        "--fixtures",
        "--funcargs",
        action="store_true",
        dest="showfixtures",
        default=False,
        help="show available fixtures, sorted by plugin appearance "
        "(fixtures with leading '_' are only shown with '-v')",
    )
    group.addoption(
        "--fixtures-per-test",
        action="store_true",
        dest="show_fixtures_per_test",
        default=False,
        help="show fixtures per test",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_files",
        type="args",
        # NOTE: default is also used in AssertionRewritingHook.
        default=["test_*.py", "*_test.py"],
        help="glob-style file patterns for Python test module discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_classes",
        type="args",
        default=["Test"],
        help="prefixes or glob names for Python test class discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_functions",
        type="args",
        default=["test"],
        help="prefixes or glob names for Python test function and method discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "disable_test_id_escaping_and_forfeit_all_rights_to_community_support",
        type="bool",
        default=False,
        help="disable string escape non-ascii characters, might cause unwanted "
        "side effects(use at your own risk)",
    )


def pytest_cmdline_main(config: Config) -> Optional[Union[int, ExitCode]]:
    if config.option.showfixtures:
        showfixtures(config)
        return 0
    if config.option.show_fixtures_per_test:
        show_fixtures_per_test(config)
        return 0
    return None


def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc: "Metafunc") -> None:
    for marker in metafunc.definition.iter_markers(name="parametrize"):
        # TODO: Fix this type-ignore (overlapping kwargs).
        metafunc.parametrize(*marker.args, **marker.kwargs, _param_mark=marker)  # type: ignore[misc]


def pytest_configure(config: Config) -> None:
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "parametrize(argnames, argvalues): call a test function multiple "
        "times passing in different arguments in turn. argvalues generally "
        "needs to be a list of values if argnames specifies only one name "
        "or a list of tuples of values if argnames specifies multiple names. "
        "Example: @parametrize('arg1', [1,2]) would lead to two calls of the "
        "decorated test function, one with arg1=1 and another with arg1=2."
        "see https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/parametrize.html for more info "
        "and examples.",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "usefixtures(fixturename1, fixturename2, ...): mark tests as needing "
        "all of the specified fixtures. see "
        "https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/fixture.html#usefixtures ",
    )


def async_warn_and_skip(nodeid: str) -> None:
    msg = "async def functions are not natively supported and have been skipped.\n"
    msg += (
        "You need to install a suitable plugin for your async framework, for example:\n"
    )
    msg += "  - pytest-asyncio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-trio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-tornasync\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-twisted"
    warnings.warn(PytestUnhandledCoroutineWarning(msg.format(nodeid)))
    skip(msg="async def function and no async plugin installed (see warnings)")


@hookimpl(trylast=True)
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem: "Function") -> Optional[object]:
    testfunction = pyfuncitem.obj
    if is_async_function(testfunction):
        async_warn_and_skip(pyfuncitem.nodeid)
    funcargs = pyfuncitem.funcargs
    testargs = {arg: funcargs[arg] for arg in pyfuncitem._fixtureinfo.argnames}
    result = testfunction(**testargs)
    if hasattr(result, "__await__") or hasattr(result, "__aiter__"):
        async_warn_and_skip(pyfuncitem.nodeid)
    return True


def pytest_collect_file(path: py.path.local, parent) -> Optional["Module"]:
    ext = path.ext
    if ext == ".py":
        if not parent.session.isinitpath(path):
            if not path_matches_patterns(
                path, parent.config.getini("python_files") + ["__init__.py"]
            ):
                return None
        ihook = parent.session.gethookproxy(path)
        module = ihook.pytest_pycollect_makemodule(
            path=path, parent=parent
        )  # type: Module
        return module
    return None


def path_matches_patterns(path: py.path.local, patterns: Iterable[str]) -> bool:
    """Returns True if path matches any of the patterns in the list of globs given."""
    return any(path.fnmatch(pattern) for pattern in patterns)


def pytest_pycollect_makemodule(path: py.path.local, parent) -> "Module":
    if path.basename == "__init__.py":
        pkg = Package.from_parent(parent, fspath=path)  # type: Package
        return pkg
    mod = Module.from_parent(parent, fspath=path)  # type: Module
    return mod


@hookimpl(trylast=True)
def pytest_pycollect_makeitem(collector: "PyCollector", name: str, obj: object):
    # nothing was collected elsewhere, let's do it here
    if safe_isclass(obj):
        if collector.istestclass(obj, name):
            return Class.from_parent(collector, name=name, obj=obj)
    elif collector.istestfunction(obj, name):
        # mock seems to store unbound methods (issue473), normalize it
        obj = getattr(obj, "__func__", obj)
        # We need to try and unwrap the function if it's a functools.partial
        # or a functools.wrapped.
        # We mustn't if it's been wrapped with mock.patch (python 2 only)
        if not (inspect.isfunction(obj) or inspect.isfunction(get_real_func(obj))):
            filename, lineno = getfslineno(obj)
            warnings.warn_explicit(
                message=PytestCollectionWarning(
                    "cannot collect %r because it is not a function." % name
                ),
                category=None,
                filename=str(filename),
                lineno=lineno + 1,
            )
        elif getattr(obj, "__test__", True):
            if is_generator(obj):
                res = Function.from_parent(collector, name=name)
                reason = "yield tests were removed in pytest 4.0 - {name} will be ignored".format(
                    name=name
                )
                res.add_marker(MARK_GEN.xfail(run=False, reason=reason))
                res.warn(PytestCollectionWarning(reason))
            else:
                res = list(collector._genfunctions(name, obj))
            return res


class PyobjMixin:
    _ALLOW_MARKERS = True

    # Function and attributes that the mixin needs (for type-checking only).
    if TYPE_CHECKING:
        name = ""  # type: str
        parent = None  # type: Optional[nodes.Node]
        own_markers = []  # type: List[Mark]

        def getparent(self, cls: Type[nodes._NodeType]) -> Optional[nodes._NodeType]:
            ...

        def listchain(self) -> List[nodes.Node]:
            ...

    @property
    def module(self):
        """Python module object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Module)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def cls(self):
        """Python class object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Class)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def instance(self):
        """Python instance object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Instance)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def obj(self):
        """Underlying Python object."""
        obj = getattr(self, "_obj", None)
        if obj is None:
            self._obj = obj = self._getobj()
            # XXX evil hack
            # used to avoid Instance collector marker duplication
            if self._ALLOW_MARKERS:
                self.own_markers.extend(get_unpacked_marks(self.obj))
        return obj

    @obj.setter
    def obj(self, value):
        self._obj = value

    def _getobj(self):
        """Gets the underlying Python object. May be overwritten by subclasses."""
        # TODO: Improve the type of `parent` such that assert/ignore aren't needed.
        assert self.parent is not None
        obj = self.parent.obj  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        return getattr(obj, self.name)

    def getmodpath(self, stopatmodule: bool = True, includemodule: bool = False) -> str:
        """ return python path relative to the containing module. """
        chain = self.listchain()
        chain.reverse()
        parts = []
        for node in chain:
            if isinstance(node, Instance):
                continue
            name = node.name
            if isinstance(node, Module):
                name = os.path.splitext(name)[0]
                if stopatmodule:
                    if includemodule:
                        parts.append(name)
                    break
            parts.append(name)
        parts.reverse()
        return ".".join(parts)

    def reportinfo(self) -> Tuple[Union[py.path.local, str], int, str]:
        # XXX caching?
        obj = self.obj
        compat_co_firstlineno = getattr(obj, "compat_co_firstlineno", None)
        if isinstance(compat_co_firstlineno, int):
            # nose compatibility
            file_path = sys.modules[obj.__module__].__file__
            if file_path.endswith(".pyc"):
                file_path = file_path[:-1]
            fspath = file_path  # type: Union[py.path.local, str]
            lineno = compat_co_firstlineno
        else:
            fspath, lineno = getfslineno(obj)
        modpath = self.getmodpath()
        assert isinstance(lineno, int)
        return fspath, lineno, modpath


class PyCollector(PyobjMixin, nodes.Collector):
    def funcnamefilter(self, name: str) -> bool:
        return self._matches_prefix_or_glob_option("python_functions", name)

    def isnosetest(self, obj: object) -> bool:
        """ Look for the __test__ attribute, which is applied by the
        @nose.tools.istest decorator
        """
        # We explicitly check for "is True" here to not mistakenly treat
        # classes with a custom __getattr__ returning something truthy (like a
        # function) as test classes.
        return safe_getattr(obj, "__test__", False) is True

    def classnamefilter(self, name: str) -> bool:
        return self._matches_prefix_or_glob_option("python_classes", name)

    def istestfunction(self, obj: object, name: str) -> bool:
        if self.funcnamefilter(name) or self.isnosetest(obj):
            if isinstance(obj, staticmethod):
                # static methods need to be unwrapped
                obj = safe_getattr(obj, "__func__", False)
            return (
                safe_getattr(obj, "__call__", False)
                and fixtures.getfixturemarker(obj) is None
            )
        else:
            return False

    def istestclass(self, obj: object, name: str) -> bool:
        return self.classnamefilter(name) or self.isnosetest(obj)

    def _matches_prefix_or_glob_option(self, option_name: str, name: str) -> bool:
        """
        checks if the given name matches the prefix or glob-pattern defined
        in ini configuration.
        """
        for option in self.config.getini(option_name):
            if name.startswith(option):
                return True
            # check that name looks like a glob-string before calling fnmatch
            # because this is called for every name in each collected module,
            # and fnmatch is somewhat expensive to call
            elif ("*" in option or "?" in option or "[" in option) and fnmatch.fnmatch(
                name, option
            ):
                return True
        return False

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        if not getattr(self.obj, "__test__", True):
            return []

        # NB. we avoid random getattrs and peek in the __dict__ instead
        # (XXX originally introduced from a PyPy need, still true?)
        dicts = [getattr(self.obj, "__dict__", {})]
        for basecls in self.obj.__class__.__mro__:
            dicts.append(basecls.__dict__)
        seen = set()  # type: Set[str]
        values = []  # type: List[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]

Thought: The add_marker implementation seems normal. The bug is that dynamically added xfail markers should cause the test failure to be treated as xfail. The code in skipping.evaluate_xfail_marks iterates over item.iter_markers(name="xfail"), which checks the node chain reversed list; iter_markers yields node, mark for reversed(listchain) and iterates node.own_markers. So dynamic markers added to own_markers should be seen.

Why did behavior change in pytest 6? There may be a change in when evaluate_xfail_marks is called: in pytest_runtest_setup it sets xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and stores it, but request.node.add_marker is called during test function runtime; in the user's test they call request.node.add_marker inside the test body, after setup, but before the assertion triggers. In pytest 5, evaluate_xfail_marks was also called in pytest_runtest_makereport or runtest_call wrapper, so dynamic addition during call would be re-evaluated. In current code, pytest_runtest_call checks item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and if None, re-evaluates. But xfailed_key was set in setup to an Xfail or None. Let's trace:

- In pytest_runtest_setup (setup stage), if not runxfail: item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item). So if no xfail initially, xfailed_key stored as None. In runtest_call (hookwrapper), it gets xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None); if xfailed is None: item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item). So it re-evaluates at call time if previously None. Great.

But dynamic addition occurs during test call, i.e., inside test function; add_marker is called while runtest_call hookwrapper is executing? Let's see runtest_call implementation: it's a hookwrapper, so plugin flow: runtest_call yields, letting test be executed between pre and post hook code. evaluate_xfail_marks is done before yield, so before test runs. But add_marker happens during test run - too late for re-evaluation before test body. However, pytest 5 probably re-evaluated xfail in makereport based on markers present at time of report generation; indeed, in pytest_runtest_makereport they check item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and if report not skipped and xfailed: they evaluate call.excinfo and set rep.outcome accordingly. But if xfailed wasn't stored earlier and was None, they won't detect dynamic addition. In pytest 5 maybe the logic was different: in makereport they re-evaluated evaluate_xfail_marks when xfailed is None. Let's open older commit? But we have this repo state; maybe in 5.x, runtest_makereport code had a branch that re-evaluated xfail marks if xfailed was None. Let's search for xfailed_key usage.

Problem: Pytest 6: Dynamically adding xfail marker in test no longer ignores failure

With pytest 5.x, we can dynamically add an xfail to a test `request` object using `request.node.add_marker(mark)` (see example below). In 5.x this treated the failing test like a a test marked statically with an `xfail`. With 6.0.0rc0 it raises.

    request.node.add_marker(mark)
    assert 0

# With 5.4.3 it showed XFAIL; with 6.0.0rc1 it shows FAIL.

- evaluate_xfail_marks(item) iterates item.iter_markers(name="xfail") and returns an Xfail dataclass when an xfail applies; it does not consult any stored state, just reads markers on the item chain.
- skipping.py defines two StoreKeys: skipped_by_mark_key and xfailed_key. Code sets item._store[xfailed_key] in pytest_runtest_setup to the result of evaluate_xfail_marks(item) (unless runxfail option set).

- pytest_runtest_makereport uses xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and then decides whether to change report.outcome/wasxfail based on xfailed and on call.excinfo. It does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks when xfailed is None.

That implies that in pytest 5.x makereport probably re-evaluated xfail marks at report time (or otherwise detected dynamic markers added during test body). In pytest 6.x makereport no longer re-evaluates when xfailed is None.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (summary)
- Problem: pytest_runtest_makereport reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) but does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if xfailed is None. That means markers added during the test body (after runtest_call's pre-yield re-evaluation) are not considered when building the final TestReport.
- Fix: In pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py), if the stored xfailed is None, re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and update item._store[xfailed_key], so dynamic xfail markers added during the test body will be detected and the final report updated accordingly.

Exact change (patch snippet)

File: src/_pytest/skipping.py

Replace (current code around the start of pytest_runtest_makereport):

    @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
    def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
        outcome = yield
        rep = outcome.get_result()
        xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
        # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
        if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
            ...

with updated code that re-evaluates when xfailed is None:

    @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
    def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
        outcome = yield
        rep = outcome.get_result()
        xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
        # If xfailed was not computed earlier (None), re-evaluate now so that
        # markers added during the test call (e.g. request.node.add_marker)
        # are respected when building the report.
        if xfailed is None:
            item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)
        # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
        if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
            ...

(One-line insertion: the if xfailed is None: ... evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and assign back to item._store.)

Rationale: This mirrors the pre-call logic in pytest_runtest_call (which re-evaluates before yield if None), but ensures evaluation also happens as late as possible (in makereport) so dynamically added markers during the call are picked up for final report generation.

The relevant "value" is item._store[xfailed_key] which gets set in pytest_runtest_setup or possibly earlier; if that store contains None it previously was only re-evaluated in pytest_runtest_call (pre-yield) — not at makereport. No numeric constants need changing.

- I verified the only related hardcoded/kind-of-constant behavior is evaluate_xfail_marks default parameters (run default True, strict uses ini). Those are unaffected.

Conclusion: No hardcoded numeric values must become dynamic — we only change control flow to re-evaluate at makereport time.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- We changed report generation to re-evaluate xfail marks; the complementary operations are:
  - pytest_runtest_setup: sets item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) before the test starts (unless --runxfail). That remains correct.
  - pytest_runtest_call: before calling test, it re-evaluates if xfailed is None and may call xfail(...) to skip run=False cases. That remains correct.
  - pytest_runtest_makereport: previously only read item._store and used that value (possibly None) to decide report outcome; we now re-evaluate if xfailed is None. The reverse operation is: if marking happens earlier (setup or fixture) we may have stored a non-None xfailed prior to call; in that case makereport will not re-evaluate because stored xfailed is not None — that is fine because it's already known.
- I re-read pytest_runtest_makereport's surrounding logic (skipping.py) to ensure no code path expects xfailed to be absent: makereport already handles call.excinfo being xfail.Exception and the unexpectedsuccess unittest path. Re-evaluation at makereport time simply enables detection of markers added during test call, with no interference with other logic like runxfail option (which makereport already skips).
- Concretely, trace of variables for the parent's complementary methods (runner.call_and_report -> makereport):
  - call_and_report calls call_runtest_hook -> runtest_call hookwrapper -> runtest_call (which re-evaluates pre-yield only if xfailed is None), then yield, test runs (dynamic add_marker may happen here), then runtest_call completes.
  - call_and_report then calls hook.pytest_runtest_makereport(item=item, call=call). Our change ensures that if runtime add_marker occurred, makereport will recompute xfailed and then act accordingly.
- No parent's method needs to be overridden because the runner's logic is still valid. We do need makereport to re-evaluate (we add that). The existing pre-call re-evaluation remains as-is (no conflict).

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"

- Concrete verification:
  - Node.add_marker continues to append to own_markers and update keywords.
  - evaluate_xfail_marks still reads item.iter_markers (which will reflect own_markers appended during test body).
  - pytest_runtest_call still re-evaluates at pre-yield if store has None (unchanged).
  - pytest_runtest_makereport now re-evaluates if store has None; this only widens detection window. If store previously had a non-None xfailed (set during setup or pre-call), we do not re-evaluate (we could but no need).
- Edge cases: If a plugin previously expected xfailed to be computed only before the call and not later, this change still only reads markers; it does not change any other fields in item._store beyond setting xfailed_key to the evaluated result (same as other places do). This is consistent with the existing design where pre-call or setup code sets that store key. So all methods continue to work.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are necessary. The parent runner's hook invocation remains the same. We only add a re-evaluation line in pytest_runtest_makereport. There is no need to override any other parent's method.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (what to change and why, full fix)

Root cause
- pytest did not re-evaluate xfail markers at the time the final TestReport is made; xfail evaluation was only done in setup and pre-call (runtest_call pre-yield). If a test adds an xfail marker dynamically during its execution (i.e., after the pre-call re-evaluation), that marker was ignored by pytest_runtest_makereport and the test failure was reported as a normal failure. Pytest 5.x apparently re-evaluated at report time (or otherwise handled this case), but pytest 6.x's skipping.py no longer re-evaluated in makereport — hence the regression.

Every location that needs change
1) Primary change: src/_pytest/skipping.py:pytest_runtest_makereport
   - Current behavior: xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and no re-evaluation if None.
   - Problem: dynamic markers added during test call are not considered.
   - Fix: If xfailed is None, call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and store the result back into item._store[xfailed_key] so makereport uses the up-to-date markers.

   Exact code change (context + patch):

   Before (excerpt):

       @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
       def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
           outcome = yield
           rep = outcome.get_result()
           xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
           # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
           if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
               ...

   After (excerpt):

       @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
       def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
           outcome = yield
           rep = outcome.get_result()
           xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
           # If xfailed wasn't previously evaluated (None), re-evaluate here
           # so that markers added during the call stage (e.g. via
           # request.node.add_marker) are taken into account when creating the
           # final report.
           if xfailed is None:
               item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)
           # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
           if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
               ...

Why this is complete (addresses every affected location)
- The only place that failed to reflect dynamically added markers at the time the report is constructed was pytest_runtest_makereport. Setup and runtest_call already re-evaluate earlier. By adding a re-evaluation in makereport we ensure:
  - Markers dynamically added during setup / fixture setup (before runtest_call yield) continue to work (they were already handled by setup/runtest_call).
  - Markers dynamically added during the test body (after runtest_call pre-yield) will now be seen at makereport time and will influence rep.wasxfail / rep.outcome, thus restoring the pytest 5.x behavior for the user case in the bug report.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None. There were no numeric constants encoding timing assumptions. The change is a control-flow change (re-evaluation at makereport), not replacing any hardcoded numbers.

Any methods that need to be added/overridden
- None. All changes are local to pytest_runtest_makereport in src/_pytest/skipping.py.

Testing notes (how this fixes the user's reproduction)
- Original minimal repro (from the report):

    def test_xfail_test(request):
        mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason="xfail")
        request.node.add_marker(mark)
        assert 0

  - With the proposed change, evaluate_xfail_marks will return an Xfail when makereport runs (because item.iter_markers(name="xfail") will see the marker added to own_markers), so the final report will mark the assertion failure as xfail rather than a normal failure.

Why this is safe
- The change mirrors the same evaluation used earlier in setup and runtest_call; it assigns the result into item._store[xfailed_key] (consistent with other places that store it).
- It only executes if item._store[xfailed_key] is None — i.e., we don't overwrite an earlier computed xfailed from setup or pre-call.
- The evaluate_xfail_marks uses the same public API for reading markers and deciding run/strict/raises, so no new invariants are introduced.

Follow-up / Optional considerations
- If desired, one could log or document that xfail can be determined as late as report-time if the markers are added during the call. But functional behavior is what users expect (pytest 5.x behavior). No behavioral change for code paths where xfail is set earlier.
- I checked tests in testing/test_skipping.py for dynamic xfail usage: the existing tests cover dynamic xfail added during fixture setup. This change extends correctness to markers added in the test body as well.

If you want, I can:
- Provide a patch file (diff) ready to apply.
- Run or suggest unit tests to add (e.g., a new test that calls request.node.add_marker inside the test body and asserts the outcome is xfailed; add to testing/test_skipping.py). Example test to add:

Summary
- Root cause: pytest_runtest_makereport didn't re-evaluate xfail when item._store[xfailed_key] was None, so markers added during the test body were ignored for the final report.
- Fix: In pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py), if item._store[xfailed_key] is None, call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and store the result before computing the report outcome.

compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: adding an xfail marker during the test body (request.node.add_marker(...)) is ignored when building the final TestReport; the failure is reported as FAIL instead of XFAIL. The wrong assumption is that xfail evaluation done in setup / pre-call is final and need not be re-evaluated at report time.
- Root cause (file/method): src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport — it reads item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) but does NOT re-run evaluate_xfail_marks(item) when that stored value is None, so markers added during the call stage are never considered.
- Proposed fix: in pytest_runtest_makereport, if item._store[xfailed_key] is None then call item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and use that value when deciding rep.wasxfail / rep.outcome. This is a single-line control-flow change in skipping.py and covers the missing case.
- Hardcoded values/constants: none need to change — this is a control-flow fix (no numeric constants).
- New/overridden methods: none required; only pytest_runtest_makereport is adjusted to re-evaluate. Other hooks (pytest_runtest_setup and pytest_runtest_call) remain unchanged.
- Investigation gaps: I did not (a) fetch the pre-6 source to show exactly how pytest 5 implemented makereport, and (b) run the test suite here. Those are recommended follow-ups to confirm historical behavior and to add a regression test.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: makereport does not re-evaluate xfail at report time (root cause) (produced in Checkpoint 1)
What: src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and proceeds without re-running evaluate_xfail_marks if xfailed is None. Why it matters: if a test adds an xfail during the call stage, item._store[xfailed_key] will have been determined earlier (None) and makereport will ignore the newly added marker and report a normal failure.

### Finding 2: add_marker makes markers immediately visible via iter_markers (evidence for fix) (produced in Checkpoint 3)
What: src/_pytest/nodes.py::Node.add_marker appends Mark to self.own_markers and updates self.keywords; nodes.iter_markers (iter_markers_with_node) yields node.own_markers first. Why it matters: evaluate_xfail_marks(item) will see markers added during the call stage if it's invoked at report time.

### Finding 3: pre-call re-evaluation is too early for markers added during test body (contributing factor) (produced in Checkpoint 3)
What: src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_call (hookwrapper) re-evaluates xfailed only before yield (before the test body runs) if item._store[xfailed_key] is None. Why it matters: markers added later (inside the test body) come after that re-evaluation, so they are missed unless makereport re-evaluates.

### Finding 4: simple, complete fix is a late re-evaluation in makereport (proposed fix) (produced in Checkpoint 4)
What: Insert in pytest_runtest_makereport:
  if xfailed is None:
      item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)
Why it matters: this restores behavior that treats failures as xfail when the marker is added during the test body; it is limited in scope and does not break other code paths.

### Finding 5: gaps to follow up (testing / history) (produced in Checkpoint 4)
What: I did not inspect the pytest-5.x code to pin the exact historical implementation difference, nor did I run the test suite here. Why it matters: a regression test should be added (test that adds xfail in test body and expects XFAIL) and historical check will confirm intent in older releases.

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/skipping.py:pytest_runtest_makereport
- Wrong assumption: makereport assumes xfail evaluation done earlier (setup or pre-call) is final and does not need re-evaluation at report time.
- Evidence: code reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and then uses that value; there is no "if xfailed is None: re-evaluate" branch. The surrounding logic already handles unexpectedsuccess and call.excinfo, but never rechecks markers added during the call.
- Related locations: src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_setup (sets item._store[xfailed_key] in setup), src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_call (re-evaluates before yield if xfailed is None).

Checkpoint(s) producing this finding: Checkpoint 1 (identify bug) and Checkpoint 4 (proposed fix).

---

### Location: src/_pytest/skipping.py:pytest_runtest_call
- Wrong assumption (as implemented earlier in the flow): the pre-call re-evaluation is sufficient to catch any dynamic marks (it runs before the test body).
- Evidence: pytest_runtest_call is a hookwrapper that, before yielding to run the test, does:
    xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
    if xfailed is None:
        item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)
  That happens before the test body, so markers added inside the test body are added too late to be picked up here.
- Related locations: src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport (should be the last chance to detect markers added during call).

Checkpoint(s) producing this finding: Checkpoint 3.

---

### Location: src/_pytest/nodes.py:Node.add_marker and iter_markers
- Wrong assumption (in original behavior being relied on): none here; this is evidence location.
- Evidence: Node.add_marker appends Mark to self.own_markers and updates self.keywords; Node.iter_markers_with_node yields node.own_markers for nodes in reversed(self.listchain()), so markers added at runtime are visible to evaluate_xfail_marks immediately if evaluate_xfail_marks is called after the add.
- Related locations: src/_pytest/mark/structures.py::MarkDecorator (how marks are created), src/_pytest/skipping.py::evaluate_xfail_marks (consumes iter_markers).

Checkpoint(s) producing this finding: Checkpoint 3.

---

### Location: src/_pytest/skipping.py:evaluate_xfail_marks
- Wrong assumption: none internal — this routine correctly inspects item.iter_markers(name="xfail") and returns Xfail (reason, run, strict, raises). But it relies on being invoked at the right time to reflect current markers.
- Evidence: evaluate_xfail_marks iterates over item.iter_markers(name="xfail") and constructs an Xfail record. Because Node.add_marker mutates own_markers, calling evaluate_xfail_marks at report time will detect markers added during the call stage.
- Related locations: src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_setup (calls it and stores result), pytest_runtest_call (re-evaluates pre-yield), pytest_runtest_makereport (needs to re-evaluate if xfailed not already set).

Checkpoint(s) producing this finding: Checkpoint 2 and Checkpoint 3.

---

Notes / Evidence locker summary
- The failing repro (from the bug report) executes request.node.add_marker(pytest.mark.xfail(...)) inside the test body, then asserts 0. Setup and pre-call checks happen before the marker is added, so unless makereport re-evaluates, the final TestReport will not see the mark. Node.add_marker and iter_markers make dynamically added markers visible; evaluate_xfail_marks will correctly detect them if invoked at report time. The minimal and safe change is adding a re-evaluation in pytest_runtest_makereport when item._store[xfailed_key] is None.

If you want, I will produce the exact diff/patch file and the minimal regression test to add (test that adds xfail inside the test body and expects XFAIL).

bug_report.Title: pytest 6: dynamically added xfail marker in test body is ignored; failing test reported as FAIL instead of XFAIL
bug_report.Description: When an xfail marker is added dynamically inside the test body via request.node.add_marker(pytest.mark.xfail(...)), pytest 6 builds a TestReport that does not treat the failure as xfail and reports a FAIL. In pytest 5.x the same pattern produced an XFAIL.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport — incorrect assumption: makereport treats earlier xfail evaluation as final. It reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and, when that stored value is None, does not re-run evaluate_xfail_marks(item). As a result, markers added during the call stage are never considered when deciding rep.wasxfail / rep.outcome.

2) src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_call — incorrect assumption: pre-call re-evaluation before yielding to run the test body is sufficient. This hookwrapper evaluates xfailed only before the test body runs (when item._store[xfailed_key] is None), so marks added inside the test body are too late to be picked up here.

3) src/_pytest/nodes.py::Node.add_marker and iter_markers — evidence location: Node.add_marker appends marks to node.own_markers and updates keywords; iter_markers yields node.own_markers, so evaluate_xfail_marks(item) will see marks added during the call stage if it is invoked after add_marker. This shows the missing behavior is solely due to makereport not re-evaluating.

4) src/_pytest/skipping.py::evaluate_xfail_marks — this routine correctly inspects item.iter_markers(name="xfail") and returns an xfail record (reason, run, strict, raises), but it must be invoked at report time to reflect markers added during the test body. The current makereport logic never invokes it when item._store.get(xfailed_key) is None.
StepsToReproduce:
Create file test_foo.py with these contents:

import pytest


def test_xfail_test(request):
    mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason="xfail")
    request.node.add_marker(mark)
    assert 0
Run pytest (example): pytest -rsx test_foo.py using pytest 6.0.0rc1 or 6.0.1rc0
Observe the test outcome reported as FAIL (not XFAIL).
For comparison, run the same file under pytest 5.4.3 and observe it reported as XFAIL.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When a test adds an xfail marker at runtime (inside the test body), a subsequent failure in that test should be reported as XFAIL (shown as xfailed) in the test output and summary.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: With pytest 6.0.0rc1/6.0.1rc0 the failing test that adds an xfail marker inside the test body is reported as FAIL; the TestReport does not mark it as xfailed.

"""Support for providing temporary directories to test functions."""
import os
import re
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

import attr
import py

from .pathlib import ensure_reset_dir
from .pathlib import LOCK_TIMEOUT
from .pathlib import make_numbered_dir
from .pathlib import make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup
from _pytest.compat import final
from _pytest.config import Config
from _pytest.deprecated import check_ispytest
from _pytest.fixtures import fixture
from _pytest.fixtures import FixtureRequest
from _pytest.monkeypatch import MonkeyPatch


@final
@attr.s(init=False)
class TempPathFactory:
    """Factory for temporary directories under the common base temp directory.

    The base directory can be configured using the ``--basetemp`` option.
    """

    _given_basetemp = attr.ib(type=Optional[Path])
    _trace = attr.ib()
    _basetemp = attr.ib(type=Optional[Path])

    def __init__(
        self,
        given_basetemp: Optional[Path],
        trace,
        basetemp: Optional[Path] = None,
        *,
        _ispytest: bool = False,
    ) -> None:
        check_ispytest(_ispytest)
        if given_basetemp is None:
            self._given_basetemp = None
        else:
            # Use os.path.abspath() to get absolute path instead of resolve() as it
            # does not work the same in all platforms (see #4427).
            # Path.absolute() exists, but it is not public (see https://bugs.python.org/issue25012).
            self._given_basetemp = Path(os.path.abspath(str(given_basetemp)))
        self._trace = trace
        self._basetemp = basetemp

    @classmethod
    def from_config(
        cls,
        config: Config,
        *,
        _ispytest: bool = False,
    ) -> "TempPathFactory":
        """Create a factory according to pytest configuration.

        :meta private:
        """
        check_ispytest(_ispytest)
        return cls(
            given_basetemp=config.option.basetemp,
            trace=config.trace.get("tmpdir"),
            _ispytest=True,
        )

    def _ensure_relative_to_basetemp(self, basename: str) -> str:
        basename = os.path.normpath(basename)
        if (self.getbasetemp() / basename).resolve().parent != self.getbasetemp():
            raise ValueError(f"{basename} is not a normalized and relative path")
        return basename

    def mktemp(self, basename: str, numbered: bool = True) -> Path:
        """Create a new temporary directory managed by the factory.

        :param basename:
            Directory base name, must be a relative path.

        :param numbered:
            If ``True``, ensure the directory is unique by adding a numbered
            suffix greater than any existing one: ``basename="foo-"`` and ``numbered=True``
            means that this function will create directories named ``"foo-0"``,
            ``"foo-1"``, ``"foo-2"`` and so on.

        :returns:
            The path to the new directory.
        """
        basename = self._ensure_relative_to_basetemp(basename)
        if not numbered:
            p = self.getbasetemp().joinpath(basename)
            p.mkdir()
        else:
            p = make_numbered_dir(root=self.getbasetemp(), prefix=basename)
            self._trace("mktemp", p)
        return p

    def getbasetemp(self) -> Path:
        """Return base temporary directory."""
        if self._basetemp is not None:
            return self._basetemp

        if self._given_basetemp is not None:
            basetemp = self._given_basetemp
            ensure_reset_dir(basetemp)
            basetemp = basetemp.resolve()
        else:
            from_env = os.environ.get("PYTEST_DEBUG_TEMPROOT")
            temproot = Path(from_env or tempfile.gettempdir()).resolve()
            user = get_user() or "unknown"
            # use a sub-directory in the temproot to speed-up
            # make_numbered_dir() call
            rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}")
            rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
            basetemp = make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup(
                prefix="pytest-", root=rootdir, keep=3, lock_timeout=LOCK_TIMEOUT
            )
        assert basetemp is not None, basetemp
        self._basetemp = t = basetemp
        self._trace("new basetemp", t)
        return t


@final
@attr.s(init=False)
class TempdirFactory:
    """Backward comptibility wrapper that implements :class:``py.path.local``
    for :class:``TempPathFactory``."""

    _tmppath_factory = attr.ib(type=TempPathFactory)

    def __init__(
        self, tmppath_factory: TempPathFactory, *, _ispytest: bool = False
    ) -> None:
        check_ispytest(_ispytest)
        self._tmppath_factory = tmppath_factory

    def mktemp(self, basename: str, numbered: bool = True) -> py.path.local:
        """Same as :meth:`TempPathFactory.mktemp`, but returns a ``py.path.local`` object."""
        return py.path.local(self._tmppath_factory.mktemp(basename, numbered).resolve())

    def getbasetemp(self) -> py.path.local:
        """Backward compat wrapper for ``_tmppath_factory.getbasetemp``."""
        return py.path.local(self._tmppath_factory.getbasetemp().resolve())


def get_user() -> Optional[str]:
    """Return the current user name, or None if getuser() does not work
    in the current environment (see #1010)."""
    import getpass

    try:
        return getpass.getuser()
    except (ImportError, KeyError):
        return None


def pytest_configure(config: Config) -> None:
    """Create a TempdirFactory and attach it to the config object.

    This is to comply with existing plugins which expect the handler to be
    available at pytest_configure time, but ideally should be moved entirely
    to the tmpdir_factory session fixture.
    """
    mp = MonkeyPatch()
    tmppath_handler = TempPathFactory.from_config(config, _ispytest=True)
    t = TempdirFactory(tmppath_handler, _ispytest=True)
    config._cleanup.append(mp.undo)
    mp.setattr(config, "_tmp_path_factory", tmppath_handler, raising=False)
    mp.setattr(config, "_tmpdirhandler", t, raising=False)


@fixture(scope="session")
def tmpdir_factory(request: FixtureRequest) -> TempdirFactory:
    """Return a :class:`_pytest.tmpdir.TempdirFactory` instance for the test session."""
    # Set dynamically by pytest_configure() above.
    return request.config._tmpdirhandler  # type: ignore


@fixture(scope="session")
def tmp_path_factory(request: FixtureRequest) -> TempPathFactory:
    """Return a :class:`_pytest.tmpdir.TempPathFactory` instance for the test session."""
    # Set dynamically by pytest_configure() above.
    return request.config._tmp_path_factory  # type: ignore


def _mk_tmp(request: FixtureRequest, factory: TempPathFactory) -> Path:
    name = request.node.name
    name = re.sub(r"[\W]", "_", name)
    MAXVAL = 30
    name = name[:MAXVAL]
    return factory.mktemp(name, numbered=True)


@fixture
def tmpdir(tmp_path: Path) -> py.path.local:
    """Return a temporary directory path object which is unique to each test
    function invocation, created as a sub directory of the base temporary
    directory.

    By default, a new base temporary directory is created each test session,
    and old bases are removed after 3 sessions, to aid in debugging. If
    ``--basetemp`` is used then it is cleared each session. See :ref:`base
    temporary directory`.

    The returned object is a `py.path.local`_ path object.

    .. _`py.path.local`: https://py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/path.html
    """
    return py.path.local(tmp_path)


@fixture
def tmp_path(request: FixtureRequest, tmp_path_factory: TempPathFactory) -> Path:
    """Return a temporary directory path object which is unique to each test
    function invocation, created as a sub directory of the base temporary
    directory.

    By default, a new base temporary directory is created each test session,
    and old bases are removed after 3 sessions, to aid in debugging. If
    ``--basetemp`` is used then it is cleared each session. See :ref:`base
    temporary directory`.

    The returned object is a :class:`pathlib.Path` object.
    """

    return _mk_tmp(request, tmp_path_factory)

class TestTempdirHandler:

class TestNumberedDir:
    PREFIX = "fun-"

    def test_make(self, tmp_path):
        for i in range(10):
            d = make_numbered_dir(root=tmp_path, prefix=self.PREFIX)
            assert d.name.startswith(self.PREFIX)
            assert d.name.endswith(str(i))

        symlink = tmp_path.joinpath(self.PREFIX + "current")
        if symlink.exists():
            # unix
            assert symlink.is_symlink()
            assert symlink.resolve() == d.resolve()

    def test_cleanup_lock_create(self, tmp_path):
        d = tmp_path.joinpath("test")
        d.mkdir()
        lockfile = create_cleanup_lock(d)
        with pytest.raises(OSError, match="cannot create lockfile in .*"):
            create_cleanup_lock(d)

        lockfile.unlink()

    def test_lock_register_cleanup_removal(self, tmp_path: Path) -> None:
        lock = create_cleanup_lock(tmp_path)

        registry: List[Callable[..., None]] = []
        register_cleanup_lock_removal(lock, register=registry.append)

        (cleanup_func,) = registry

        assert lock.is_file()

        cleanup_func(original_pid="intentionally_different")

        assert lock.is_file()

        cleanup_func()

        assert not lock.exists()

        cleanup_func()

        assert not lock.exists()

    def _do_cleanup(self, tmp_path: Path) -> None:
        self.test_make(tmp_path)
        cleanup_numbered_dir(
            root=tmp_path,
            prefix=self.PREFIX,
            keep=2,
            consider_lock_dead_if_created_before=0,
        )

    def test_cleanup_keep(self, tmp_path):
        self._do_cleanup(tmp_path)
        a, b = (x for x in tmp_path.iterdir() if not x.is_symlink())
        print(a, b)

    def test_cleanup_locked(self, tmp_path):
        p = make_numbered_dir(root=tmp_path, prefix=self.PREFIX)

        create_cleanup_lock(p)

        assert not pathlib.ensure_deletable(
            p, consider_lock_dead_if_created_before=p.stat().st_mtime - 1
        )
        assert pathlib.ensure_deletable(
            p, consider_lock_dead_if_created_before=p.stat().st_mtime + 1
        )

    def test_cleanup_ignores_symlink(self, tmp_path):
        the_symlink = tmp_path / (self.PREFIX + "current")
        attempt_symlink_to(the_symlink, tmp_path / (self.PREFIX + "5"))
        self._do_cleanup(tmp_path)

    def test_removal_accepts_lock(self, tmp_path):
        folder = make_numbered_dir(root=tmp_path, prefix=self.PREFIX)
        create_cleanup_lock(folder)
        maybe_delete_a_numbered_dir(folder)
        assert folder.is_dir()


class TestRmRf:
    def test_rm_rf(self, tmp_path):
        adir = tmp_path / "adir"
        adir.mkdir()
        rm_rf(adir)

        assert not adir.exists()

        adir.mkdir()
        afile = adir / "afile"
        afile.write_bytes(b"aa")

        rm_rf(adir)
        assert not adir.exists()

    def test_rm_rf_with_read_only_file(self, tmp_path):
        """Ensure rm_rf can remove directories with read-only files in them (#5524)"""
        fn = tmp_path / "dir/foo.txt"
        fn.parent.mkdir()

        fn.touch()

        self.chmod_r(fn)

        rm_rf(fn.parent)

        assert not fn.parent.is_dir()

    def chmod_r(self, path):
        mode = os.stat(str(path)).st_mode
        os.chmod(str(path), mode & ~stat.S_IWRITE)

    def test_rm_rf_with_read_only_directory(self, tmp_path):
        """Ensure rm_rf can remove read-only directories (#5524)"""
        adir = tmp_path / "dir"
        adir.mkdir()

        (adir / "foo.txt").touch()
        self.chmod_r(adir)

        rm_rf(adir)

        assert not adir.is_dir()

    def test_on_rm_rf_error(self, tmp_path: Path) -> None:
        adir = tmp_path / "dir"
        adir.mkdir()

        fn = adir / "foo.txt"
        fn.touch()
        self.chmod_r(fn)

        # unknown exception
        with pytest.warns(pytest.PytestWarning):
            exc_info1 = (None, RuntimeError(), None)
            on_rm_rf_error(os.unlink, str(fn), exc_info1, start_path=tmp_path)
            assert fn.is_file()

        # we ignore FileNotFoundError
        exc_info2 = (None, FileNotFoundError(), None)
        assert not on_rm_rf_error(None, str(fn), exc_info2, start_path=tmp_path)

        # unknown function
        with pytest.warns(
            pytest.PytestWarning,
            match=r"^\(rm_rf\) unknown function None when removing .*foo.txt:\nNone: ",
        ):
            exc_info3 = (None, PermissionError(), None)

1d) Plain statement of the bug and primary responsibility
- Bug: When getpass.getuser() returns a username containing path separator characters (e.g. "contoso\john_doe"), the basetemp path computed by TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() becomes multi-level (pytest-of-contoso/john_doe). The code calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) without parents=True, so mkdir fails if an intermediate directory does not exist -> FileNotFoundError on Windows.
- Primary responsible class/method: TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() in src/_pytest/tmpdir.py constructs rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") using the raw username; the helper get_user() returns the raw getpass.getuser() and is the upstream source of unsafe characters.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (summary)
- Sanitize the username returned by get_user() so it cannot inject path separators or other characters illegal/unsafe for filesystem names.
- Be defensive when creating the "pytest-of-{user}" directory: use mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) so that if some separator is still present (for unexpected platforms), directory creation doesn't fail. (This is defensive and will not change behavior for normal usernames.)
- Add a unit test verifying get_user sanitizes usernames with backslashes.

Concrete code changes
1) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py
- Modify get_user() to sanitize the username:
  - Trim the raw name returned by getpass.getuser()
  - Replace any character that is not in the safe set [A-Za-z0-9._-] with underscore. (This removes slashes/backslashes and other problematic characters.)
- Update rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) to rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) in getbasetemp to be defensive.

Exact code patch (conceptual; lines referenced from the file I read):

In getbasetemp (change at line ~117):
- Old:
    rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}")
    rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
- New:
    rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}")
    # create the rootdir; be defensive and allow intermediate directories if user
    # contains path separators (shouldn't after sanitization, but guard anyway)
    rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

In get_user (replace old implementation at line ~151):
- Old:
    def get_user() -> Optional[str]:
        import getpass
        try:
            return getpass.getuser()
        except (ImportError, KeyError):
            return None
- New:
    def get_user() -> Optional[str]:
        import getpass
        try:
            raw = getpass.getuser()
        except (ImportError, KeyError):
            return None
        # sanitize username for safe use in filenames: allow ASCII letters, digits,
        # dot, underscore and hyphen; replace all other characters with underscore.
        # This removes path separators like '\\' and '/' which would otherwise be
        # interpreted as directory separators on some platforms.
        return re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9._-]+", "_", raw)

Notes:
- re is already imported at top of tmpdir.py.
- This returns None the same way as before if getpass.getuser() could not be queried.

2) testing/test_tmpdir.py
- Add a test to ensure get_user sanitizes backslashes. Example to add near existing get_user tests:

    def test_get_user_sanitizes_backslash(monkeypatch):
        import getpass
        # Simulate getpass.getuser() returning a domain\user name.
        monkeypatch.setattr("getpass.getuser", lambda: r"contoso\\john_doe")
        assert get_user() == "contoso_john_doe"

(Use raw string with double backslash in the lambda to represent a single backslash.)

Rationale: this test ensures our sanitization addresses the bug scenario reported (usernames like contoso\john_doe).

Now work through the mandatory questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current assumption changed: the code assumed that getpass.getuser() returns a username that is safe to use as a single filesystem component (no path separators or illegal characters). The fix removes that assumption by sanitizing the username before using it in filenames.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- Investigated hardcoded values:
  - The "pytest-of-" prefix (tmpdir.getbasetemp uses f"pytest-of-{user}") encodes the assumption that user is a single path component. If user contains separators, the composed string has separators.
  - The call to rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) (without parents=True) assumes the path passed is a single-level directory and that parent exists; this encodes the same assumption.
- Are these values still correct after the fix?
  - "pytest-of-" prefix: still correct; it remains a prefix for the username used as a subdir. Since username is sanitized, no separators will be present and prefix use is fine.
  - rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True): after sanitizing user this call will succeed (no intermediate components). Additionally, I've made it defensive and changed to rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) to avoid the previous failure in case something slips through. So I addressed both places that encoded the unsafe assumption.

3rd-party/hardcoded constants in pathlib.py (LOCK_TIMEOUT, "pytest-") are not dependent on username structure and therefore remain correct; they require no change.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The complementary operations to "creating basetemp" are the cleanup logic in make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup and cleanup_numbered_dir which discover previous runs by looking for numbered directories under the rootdir with a given prefix, and then parsing suffix numbers.
- Since get_user() previously could create nested directories if it contained backslashes, older/bad basetemp directories (if any) could have been created in nested form or failed to be created. Our fix sanitizes new basetemps, so cleanup logic that searches for numbered dirs under temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") will succeed for sanitized names.
- I re-read make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup and cleanup_numbered_dir in src/_pytest/pathlib.py:
  - They operate on the rootdir Path passed in and look for entries whose names start with a prefix (case-insensitive find_prefixed).
  - Under the new sanitized naming, these routines behave correctly and do not need modification.
- Concretely, trace through complementary read/cleanup code with the primary class's values:
  - rootdir = temproot / f"pytest-of-{sanitized_user}"
  - make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup(root=rootdir, prefix="pytest-"...)
  - make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup will use make_numbered_dir on that rootdir; find_prefixed and numeric parsing will work as intended because the created folder names are of the form "pytest-{n}" within that rootdir.
- Because we also changed mkdir(parents=True), even if user had slashes, rootdir would be created as nested directories. make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup will then operate in the right directory. So both creation and cleanup work consistently.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
Check each method in TempPathFactory:
- __init__: unchanged — still stores given_basetemp / trace / basetemp.
- from_config: unchanged.
- _ensure_relative_to_basetemp: unchanged — it uses getbasetemp() but only for verifying basename is relative; sanitization of user does not affect its logic.
- mktemp: unchanged — uses getbasetemp() then creates or calls make_numbered_dir. Works the same because basetemp exists and is a directory.
- getbasetemp: changed in two small ways:
  - It now uses sanitized username (via modified get_user).
  - rootdir.mkdir(...) changed to parents=True defensively.
  The method still returns a Path to a basetemp and sets self._basetemp. All returned values and calls to _trace are unchanged. No other method relies on the exact string value of user except for constructing the path, so they continue to work.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required.
- However, I added a defensive change to getbasetemp's rootdir.mkdir(..., parents=True, exist_ok=True). This is not a new method but a small behavioral change to directory creation to help avoid the error even if an unexpected separator appears.
- Parent methods that perform complementary operations (cleanup_numbered_dir, make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup) were reviewed and do not need overriding; they will interact correctly with the sanitized directory structure.
- The TempdirFactory wrapper continues to work as it delegates to TempPathFactory and doesn't assume anything about the username.

Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause, all locations to change, and exact fixes

Root cause
- The code used the raw result of getpass.getuser() in the construction of a filesystem directory name (f"pytest-of-{user}") without sanitization. If getpass.getuser() contained path separators (e.g. domain\username) or other characters invalid or meaningful for filenames, Path.joinpath interpreted them as path components or invalid characters, leading to either nested directories or invalid names. Because the code called rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) without parents=True, creation failed with FileNotFoundError when intermediate directories were implied but not present.

Every location that needs to change (and why)
1) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py: get_user()
   - Current code: returns raw getpass.getuser()
   - Assumption encoded: username is safe to use directly in a single filename component.
   - Fix: sanitize the returned username by replacing any character not in a safe set [A-Za-z0-9._-] with underscore.
   - Reason: ensures the username cannot contain path separators or other characters that would create nested paths or invalid filenames.

2) src/_pytest/tmpdir.py: TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() (rootdir.mkdir call)
   - Current code: rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
   - Assumption encoded: rootdir is a single-level directory and the parent (temproot) exists. If user had an internal separator, rootdir might represent nested directories and mkdir w/o parents would fail.
   - Fix: rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True). This is defensive: if anything unexpected slips through sanitization, creation succeeds rather than failing with FileNotFoundError. It preserves current behavior for normal usernames.

3) testing/test_tmpdir.py: add unit test
   - Current tests exercise get_user in fallback cases but do not test usernames with separators.
   - Fix: add a test that monkeypatches getpass.getuser to return "contoso\john_doe" and assert that get_user() returns "contoso_john_doe". This documents and ensures the behavior remains stable.

Why not change pathlib.py
- The cleanup and directory-numbering logic in src/_pytest/pathlib.py assume directories are named with the given prefixes and numeric suffixes. They do not make assumptions about the username. Sanitizing input and ensuring rootdir exists makes the temporary directory creation consistent with the expectations of make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup and cleanup_numbered_dir, so no change is needed there.
- The hardcoded LOCK_TIMEOUT and "pytest-" prefix are unrelated to the username issue and remain valid.

All hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None. The values inspected (LOCK_TIMEOUT, "pytest-", "pytest-of-") are independent of username character encoding. The only change is sanitizing input (username), not altering the constants.

Summary of patch (diff-style description)

- src/_pytest/tmpdir.py
  - Change:
    rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}")
    rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
  - To:
    rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}")
    rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

  - Change get_user() from returning raw getpass.getuser() to sanitized:
    def get_user() -> Optional[str]:
        import getpass
        try:
            raw = getpass.getuser()
        except (ImportError, KeyError):
            return None
        return re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9._-]+", "_", raw)

- testing/test_tmpdir.py
  - Add:
    def test_get_user_sanitizes_backslash(monkeypatch):
        import getpass
        monkeypatch.setattr("getpass.getuser", lambda: r"contoso\\john_doe")
        assert get_user() == "contoso_john_doe"

Notes, rationale, and side-effect analysis
- Sanitizing only changes the name used for the basetemp subdirectory. It does not change semantics of the tmpdir/tmp_path fixtures from the test author's perspective besides fixing failures caused by illegal username characters.
- This is backward compatible because the sanitized username will be predictable and filesystem-safe. Existing users without domain separators will see the same or trivially identical names.
- I chose a conservative safe character set: ASCII letters and digits plus dot, underscore, and hyphen. This avoids cross-platform filename issues (slash/backslash/colon etc.). One could choose a Unicode-aware \w set, but that could include characters that might be problematic on some filesystems — I kept it conservative.
- rootdir.mkdir(parents=True) is defensive and will not change behavior in normal cases; it prevents the earlier FileNotFoundError in the event any path-separator-like characters appear.
- Tests: the added test guards the behavior for the reported case. No existing test should fail because get_user() previously returned a value and now returns a sanitized version; existing tests didn't assert an exact username string, only presence/None in fallback cases.

If you want, I can produce the exact patch (git diff) for both files (tmpdir.py and testing/test_tmpdir.py) ready for application.

===HIGH_LEVEL===

- What is the bug / wrong assumption: pytest constructs basetemp using the raw value from getpass.getuser() (used as f"pytest-of-{user}") and assumes that returned username is safe as a single filename component. On Windows a domain username like "contoso\john_doe" contains a backslash which is interpreted as a path separator, producing a multi-level path and causing mkdir(..., exist_ok=True) to fail with FileNotFoundError. (See src/_pytest/tmpdir.py getbasetemp and get_user.) (Checkpoint 1)
- Root cause and location: get_user() returns the raw getpass.getuser() (src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:def get_user()) and TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() constructs rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") then calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) — the combination permits path-separator characters in the directory name. (Checkpoint 1,2)
- Proposed fix strategy (complete): sanitize the username returned by get_user() to remove/replace path separators and other filesystem-unsafe characters (exact regex below), and make directory creation defensive by using rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True). Update/add a unit test that simulates a domain\user and asserts the sanitized result. This addresses all call sites because get_user() is the single upstream source. (Checkpoint 4)
- Hardcoded values/constants to change: no global constants need changing. Instead change runtime use of get_user() (sanitization) and the mkdir call. The "pytest-of-" prefix and LOCK_TIMEOUT remain valid. (Checkpoint 3,4)
- New/overridden methods needed: none. Change is to existing function get_user() and to TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() mkdir call; add a test in testing/test_tmpdir.py. (Checkpoint 4)
- Investigation gaps / risks remaining: the agent did not run a Windows-specific concrete trace of pathlib.Path.joinpath semantics with actual backslash characters (i.e., did not execute live I/O) and did not evaluate non-ASCII / Unicode username edge-cases; those should be covered by CI on Windows and by an additional test for Unicode usernames if desired. (Checkpoint 4c / 4e)

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: get_user() returns raw, unsafe username
What: src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:def get_user() returns getpass.getuser() unchanged, encoding the assumption that the username is a single safe filename component. Why it matters: raw "contoso\john_doe" includes "\": leads to a path with two components. (Found in file search and inspection.) (Checkpoint 1,2)

### Finding 2: basetemp path construction assumes safe username
What: src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:TempPathFactory.getbasetemp() constructs rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") then calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True). Why it matters: if f"pytest-of-{user}" contains a path separator the resulting Path represents nested directories but mkdir is called without parents=True, causing FileNotFoundError. Evidence: report stack trace showing WindowsPath('.../pytest-of-contoso/john_doe') and pathlib.py traceback. (Checkpoint 1,2)

### Finding 3: make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup and cleanup logic are compatible with the fix
What: src/_pytest/pathlib.py functions (make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup, make_numbered_dir, cleanup_numbered_dir) operate inside the rootdir Path passed from tmpdir; they don't assume anything about user encoding and will work once username is sanitized. Why it matters: ensures fix is complete — no changes required in pathlib.py. (Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 4: Defensive mkdir change recommended
What: Even after sanitization, change rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True) -> rootdir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) to avoid failing if unexpected separators remain. Why it matters: prevents the exact reported failure even if sanitization misses an edge-case. (Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 5: Tests missing for domain\user and Unicode cases (gap)
What: testing/test_tmpdir.py contains tests for get_user() only for fallback/None cases; there is no test asserting sanitization of usernames with backslashes or for Unicode usernames. Why it matters: without tests the regression can reappear or Unicode handling may be broken by the chosen sanitization. Action: add test_get_user_sanitizes_backslash and consider a Unicode test. (Checkpoint 2,4)

### Finding 6: No other call sites use get_user()
What: repository search found get_user() used only in src/_pytest/tmpdir.py and testing/test_tmpdir.py, so sanitizing get_user() centrally covers all uses. Why it matters: ensures the fix is localized and complete. (Checkpoint 3)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:TempPathFactory.getbasetemp
- **Wrong assumption**: Assumes f"pytest-of-{user}" is a single path component (user contains no path separators or illegal filename chars).

  - Code constructs: rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") followed by rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True). (src/_pytest/tmpdir.py; observed lines around 114–119 in the investigation.)
  - Stack trace from bug report: WindowsPath('C:/.../Temp/pytest-of-contoso/john_doe') and FileNotFoundError originating from pathlib.Path.mkdir because intermediate directory 'pytest-of-contoso' did not exist.
- **Related locations**: src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:def get_user (upstream input), src/_pytest/pathlib.py:make_numbered_dir_with_cleanup (downstream use of rootdir), testing/test_tmpdir.py (tests).

(Checkpoint 1 produced the identification of this failing mkdir and stack trace; Checkpoint 2 confirmed code location.)

### Location: src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:get_user

bug_report.Title: tmpdir creation fails when getpass.getuser() returns a username with path-separator characters (e.g. domain\user) on Windows
bug_report.Description: pytest tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory use getpass.getuser() unchanged to build the basetemp directory name. If the returned username contains path-separator or filesystem-unsafe characters (for example a Windows domain username like "contoso\john_doe"), the constructed path becomes multi-component and directory creation fails on Windows with FileNotFoundError.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:def get_user: get_user() returns getpass.getuser() unchanged, encoding the assumption that the username is a single filesystem-safe filename component. This is false for domain usernames like "contoso\john_doe". Correct relationship: the username must be converted to a filesystem-safe single component (investigation shows the intended sanitization formula: sanitized = re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9._-]+", "_", raw)).

2) Incorrect assumption in src/_pytest/tmpdir.py:TempPathFactory.getbasetemp: code constructs rootdir = temproot.joinpath(f"pytest-of-{user}") and calls rootdir.mkdir(exist_ok=True), assuming the path argument is a single directory level whose parent already exists. If user contains path separators the Path represents nested components and mkdir(..., exist_ok=True) fails. Correct relationship: either ensure the component contains no separators (sanitize username) or create intermediate directories when the Path contains multiple components (the investigation recommends mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) as the defensive relationship).

3) Test coverage gap in testing/test_tmpdir.py: tests exercise get_user() only for fallback/None cases and do not assert sanitization for usernames containing separators or for Unicode usernames. Because get_user() is the single upstream source (search found no other call sites), missing tests allow regression or unintended Unicode behavior to slip in.
StepsToReproduce: Create a minimal test file: echo def test_tmpdir(tmpdir):>test_tmp.py && echo   pass>>test_tmp.py
From Windows cmd set an environment username containing a backslash: set LOGNAME=contoso\john_doe
Run pytest: py.test test_tmp.py
Observe the failure when tmpdir/tmpdir_factory attempts to create basetemp (FileNotFoundError referencing a path like '.../pytest-of-contoso/john_doe').
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: pytest should create temporary root directories for tests (tmpdir/tmpdir_factory/tmp_path_factory) successfully regardless of getpass.getuser() output. The username used in the basetemp directory name should be presented as a single filesystem-safe directory component (domain separators and other illegal filename characters replaced) so directory creation succeeds on Windows and other platforms.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Attempting to create a temp directory fails with FileNotFoundError on Windows. The error shows the basetemp path split into components (for example '.../Temp/pytest-of-contoso/john_doe'), and pathlib.Path.mkdir raises FileNotFoundError because an intermediate parent directory does not exist.
